chapters.summary: Council staff welcomes attendees to the joint hearing of the Committees on Women and Gender Equity and General Welfare, asks attendees to silence devices and not approach the dais.
chapters.title: Staff housekeeping and hearing opening announcements

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to today's New York City Council hearing for the Committees on Women and Gender Equity, joint with General Welfare.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: At this time, we ask that you please silence all cell phones and electronic devices to minimize disruptions throughout the hearing.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: If you wish to testify, please see a sergeant in arms for assistance.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: At any time throughout today's proceeding, please do not approach the dais.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: We thank you for your kind cooperation.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chairs, we are ready to begin.

chapters.summary: Chair Farías introduces the hearing on LGBTQ+ housing instability, connecting Pride Month to the Stonewall riots and the origins of the LGBTQ+ liberation movement among housing-insecure people.
chapters.title: Chair Farías: Stonewall history and LGBTQ+ housing connection

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Good morning, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Amanda Farias, Chair of the Committee on Women and Gender Equity.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'm excited to be joined by the Chair of the Committee on General Welfare, Councilmember Crystal Hudson, for today's hearing on housing instability among LGBTQ plus individuals in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: As we celebrate Pride Month here in New York, it is important to recognize the reality of the LGBT plus community's ongoing struggles, one of them being that in the United States,

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: LGBTQ plus individuals are more likely to face housing insecurity than non-LGBTQ plus people.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: When we look back at the origins of pride at the 1969 Stonewall riots, we are reminded that the modern LGBTQ plus liberation movement was largely formed by housing insecure people.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: The Stonewall Bar was a haven for young LGBTQ plus individuals who had nowhere else to go.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Housing injustice and LGBTQ plus rights have been intertwined since the beginning.

chapters.summary: Farías details how nearly half of NYC's homeless youth identify as LGBTQ+, elders face discrimination in assisted living, and shelter staff often fail to honor gender identity in placements.
chapters.title: Chair Farías: Current challenges for LGBTQ+ youth and elders

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Today, LGBTQ plus individuals continue to face challenges in accessing safe, stable, and affordable housing.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Often this begins tragically early when many young people are forced to leave their homes due to family rejection or unsupportive environments.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Almost half of all homeless youth in New York City identified as LGBTQ+, and one in four have experienced homelessness or housing insecurity at one point in their lives.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: But these challenges often persist throughout an LGBTQ plus person's life with

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: LGBTQ plus elders facing high risks of being turned away from or being charged or being charges higher charged with higher rents at independent or assisted living centers as nationwide 48%

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: of LGBTQ plus seniors experience discrimination when applying for housing for LGBTQ plus individuals the challenges don't end when they gain access to shelter services many experience discrimination harassment and even violence when seeking support furthermore

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: if staff are not equipped to serve LGBTQ plus people it can often lead to additional harm especially when staff fails to honor a person's gender identity and houses a person based on their sex assigned at birth rather than where the person feels the safest

chapters.summary: Farías frames NYC as a progressive leader with the largest LGBTQ+ metro population, outlines questions for the administration on housing resources and shelter staff training, and invites Chair Hudson to speak.
chapters.title: Chair Farías: NYC leadership and questions for administration

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: New York City has historically been a progressive leader and a safe haven for individuals of all genders and sexual orientations, boasting the largest population of LGBTQ plus people of any U.S.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Metro area.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Our city needs to bolster our supportive housing services for LGBTQ plus people and the development of more affordable and supportive housing options for this community.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: So today we have

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: We'll ask the administration how you are working to address housing instability among LGBTQ plus individuals in New York City, what specific housing resources are available to the LGBTQ plus community, and what trainings are

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: available for people working in shelters around engagement with LGBT plus individuals.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Now I'd like to invite Chair Hudson to deliver her opening remarks.

chapters.summary: Chair Hudson introduces herself, thanks Chair Farías for allyship, and acknowledges council members Avilés, Ung, Morano, Wilson, and El DeBall in attendance.
chapters.title: Chair Hudson: Introduction and thanks

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to today's oversight hearing.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I'm Crystal Hudson, General Welfare Committee Chair, and my pronouns are she, her.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I would like to also thank Councilmember Fideas, who, from my own personal experience as well as my professional experience working with her, puts allyship into action.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And I'd like to thank the Committee on Women and Gender Equity for joining the Committee on General Welfare in holding this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Additionally, we are joined by council members Aviles, Ung, Murano on Zoom, Wilson, and El DeBall.

chapters.summary: Hudson cites statistics showing 55% of LGBTQ+ youth and 67% of transgender clients were forced out or ran away due to identity, and 70% of trans respondents in shelters reported mistreatment.
chapters.title: Chair Hudson: Disproportionate impact and barriers

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Despite New York City's reputation as a place of inclusion, LGBTQ plus individuals, particularly transgender and gender nonconforming people, LGBTQ plus youth and LGBTQ plus people of color remain disproportionately impacted by housing insecurity.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Many are forced from their homes due to family rejection.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Many face barriers to accessing safe and affirming shelters, and many encounter discrimination when seeking permanent affordable housing.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Compounded by rising rents, a shortage of affordable housing, and economic inequality that continues to affect our most vulnerable neighbors, the challenges can feel insurmountable.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: New York City's overall homelessness crisis continues to grow, with tens of thousands of people residing in shelters nightly.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Within this broader crisis, LGBTQ plus individuals, particularly transgender people, youth, and people of color, face unique barriers to obtaining and maintaining stable housing.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: In the LGBTQ plus homeless youth provider survey, providers reported that being forced out by parents or they ran away because of their sexual orientation, gender identity, or gender expression was a primary reason for homelessness in over 55% of their LGBTQ

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: youth clients and over 67% of their transgender clients, with general family issues underlying homelessness for an additional...

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: 16.5% of LGBTQ+, and 7.3% of transgender clients.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Among respondents to the most recent U.S.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Transgender survey, 70% of respondents who experienced homelessness and stayed in a shelter the previous year reported some form of mistreatment, including being harassed, assaulted, or kicked out because of being transgender.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: More than 36% of respondents who experienced homelessness in the previous year

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: avoided staying in shelter because they feared being mistreated as a transgender person.

chapters.summary: Hudson calls for policies addressing systemic inequities, not just awareness, and thanks committee staff including Aminta Kilowan, Penina Rosenberg, Justin Campos, and others for their work on the hearing.
chapters.title: Chair Hudson: Need for tangible action and policy

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: We recognize that addressing housing instability among LGBTQ plus New Yorkers requires more than awareness.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: It requires tangible action.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: As lawmakers and governmental officials, we need to be thoughtfully and intentionally creating policies that not only respond to immediate needs, but also address the systemic inequities

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: that place LGBTQ plus New Yorkers at greater risk of homelessness.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: We hope that today's hearing will inform an assessment of how we can reduce the barriers that LGBTQ plus people experience when it comes to achieving housing stability and financial sustainability more generally.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you for your commitment to this important issue, and I look forward to today's discussion.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And I'd like to conclude by thanking the committee staff for their work on this hearing, Aminta Kilowan, Penina Rosenberg, Justin Campos, Farah Rahman, and Elizabeth Childers-Garcia.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And I'd also like to thank my staff, Andrew Wright, Elika Ruan-Tan, and Demi Brown.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Farías thanks committee staff, reminds witnesses to fill out witness slips, limits testimony to two minutes, notes no voting today, and states in-person witnesses testify before Zoom participants.
chapters.title: Chair Farías: Testimony rules and staff acknowledgments

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Hudson.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'd like to thank the members of the Women and Gender Equity Committee and the General Welfare Committee who've joined us today.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'd also like to thank Committee Staff Julia Goldsmith, Pinkham, Katie Selena, and Ali Stauffer, as well as my own staff, Dan Curtin and Josephine Karras, for their work on today's hearing.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I would like to remind everyone who wishes to testify in person today that you must fill out a witness slip, which you can find on the desk of the sergeant at arms in the back of the room near the entrance.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Please fill out the slip even if you have already registered in advance that you will be testifying in person today.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: If you wish to testify on any of the legislation, please indicate on the witness slip whether you are here to testify in favor of or in opposition to the legislation.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I also want to point out that we will not be voting on any legislation today.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: To allow as many people as possible to testify, testimony will be limited to two minutes per person, whether you're testifying in person or on Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'm also going to ask my colleagues to limit their questions and comments to five minutes.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Please note that witnesses...

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: who are here in person will testify before those who are signed onto the Zoom webinar.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'm now going to turn it over to the Women and Gender Equity Committee Council to administer the oath.

chapters.summary: Council staff administers the affirmation to all witnesses, who affirm to tell the truth and respond honestly to council member questions.
chapters.title: Oath administration for witnesses

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: In accordance with the rules of the council, I'll administer the affirmation.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I will call on you each individually for a response.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please raise your right hand.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before these committees and respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: As a reminder to all of our witnesses, please state your name prior to your testimony for the record.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: If anyone here today requires an accessible version of the presentation given today at today's hearing, please email testimony at council.myc.gov, and we'll turn it over to you folks to begin.

chapters.summary: Taylor Brown, inaugural director of the Mayor's Office of LGBTQIA Affairs, introduces the office's mission to address systemic barriers preventing LGBTQIA New Yorkers from thriving, with housing as foundational to well-being.
chapters.title: Taylor Brown: Introduction and mission of Mayor's Office of LGBTQIA Affairs

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Okay, mic check.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: All right, can everyone hear me?

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: My name is Taylor Brown.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: I use she, her pronouns.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: I serve as the inaugural director of the New York City Mayor's Office of Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Transgender, Queer, Intersex, and Asexual Affairs.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Thank you to the members of the Committee on General Welfare and Committee on Women and Gender Equity for the opportunity to testify on the critical issues of housing instability and homelessness among LGBTQIA New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: I appreciate the City Council's attention to these issues and desire to work towards meaningful solutions.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: I appreciate the testimony and ongoing partnership with my fellow agencies represented here today and across the administration, as well as the community-based organizations, advocates, and community members working every day to ensure that all New Yorkers have access to safe, affordable,

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: and stable housing and supportive services.

chapters.summary: Brown describes how transgender, nonbinary, youth, older, disabled, and LGBTQI people of color face disparate barriers to housing, driven by intersecting inequities rather than a single factor.
chapters.title: Taylor Brown: Housing insecurity disparities for LGBTQI New Yorkers

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Part of the broad mission of the Mayor's Office of LGBTQIA Affairs is to address both emerging and systemic barriers that prevent New Yorkers from thriving across all facets of life.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: We recognize that for every person, access to housing and housing stability are foundational to health, safety, economic opportunity, and overall well-being.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: We also understand that housing insecurity and homelessness do not impact all populations equally.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: LGBTQI New Yorkers, particularly transgender, gender nonconforming and nonbinary people, youth populations, older populations, those living with

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: disabilities, and LGBTQI people of color have and continue to experience disparate barriers that place them at increased vulnerability of housing insecurity and homelessness.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: The seriousness of these issues is reflected in the testimony and data provided by our partner agencies and service providers.

chapters.summary: Brown traces how longstanding legal and social discrimination against LGBTQIA people resulted in disparate rates of poverty, unemployment, health care barriers, and criminalization that compound housing insecurity.
chapters.title: Taylor Brown: Legacy of discrimination and contemporary disparities

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Their testimony demonstrates that housing insecurity and homelessness among LGBTQI New Yorkers is not the result of a single factor, but the culmination of a myriad intersecting and entrenched inequities.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: These inequities have been further exacerbated by current economic, market, and political challenges facing us all.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: While every person's circumstances are unique, it is well established that educational opportunities, employment opportunities, and access to health care are equalizers and key determinants in a person's overall quality of life and ability to lead a stable, meaningful, fulfilled, and independent life.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: But for most of our history, discrimination based on sexual orientation, gender, sex, gender identity, gender expression, and LGBTQIA status was permissible both socially and legally.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: This discrimination was perpetrated and condoned explicitly and implicitly by individuals, the private sector, and the public sector, including federal, state, and local governments.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: The consequences of longstanding discrimination are borne out in the multitude of disparities facing LGBTQI New Yorkers today.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Longstanding discrimination has resulted in LGBTQI people experiencing disparate rates of poverty, unemployment, and lack of employment opportunities, income inequality, educational limitations, barriers to medically necessary health care, social and economic immobility,

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: political and social exclusion, hostility and violence, and criminalization.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: These issues have compounding and collateral consequences, including direct implication and impacts in the context of housing insecurity and homelessness.

chapters.summary: Brown describes surging affordability crises, stagnant wages, and housing market failures, compounded by the Trump administration's weaponization of federal government to roll back LGBTQIA legal protections.
chapters.title: Taylor Brown: Economic climate and federal hostility

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: The legacy of discrimination that informs contemporary disparities in the areas of housing insecurity, homelessness, and other critical areas of life must also be understood in the context of the current economic and political climates.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Across our city, state, and nation, most people are waking up every day to ever-declining economic conditions and forecasts not seen in decades.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: At a societal level, we are collectively experiencing surging affordability crises across the spectrum of life, increased levels of indebtedness, stagnant wages and employment opportunities, the inability to save money,

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: and longstanding issues in the housing market that further drive disparities in housing insecurity and homelessness for LGBTQ New Yorkers and other minority communities.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: These longstanding market issues include a lack of housing supply in both rental and ownership markets, skyrocketing rental costs, affordable housing deficits, lending barriers and costs,

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: barriers to home ownership, unfair private sector housing and real estate practices, and lack of meaningful governmental solutions and intervention over decades to address systemic issues.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Concurrently, the Trump administration has weaponized almost every part of the federal government to attack and roll back the progress we have made in establishing LGBTQIA legal equality and government-driven intervention models in redressing systemic discrimination and its collateral consequences.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: These macroeconomic conditions and federal political hostility compound and exacerbate the underlying causes of and disparities in housing insecurity and homelessness facing LGBTQ New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: As well as other minority and marginalized communities.

chapters.summary: Brown outlines the office's phased approach: agency-wide regulatory and policy audits, programmatic evaluations, stakeholder convenings, and executive-level strategy to eliminate disparities through interagency coordination.
chapters.title: Taylor Brown: Strategic planning and implementation approach

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Against this backdrop, the Mayor's Office of LGBTQIA Affairs, housed within the New York City Mayor's Office of Equity and Racial Justice and within the portfolio of the New York City Deputy Mayor for Economic Justice,

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: has already begun its broad strategic thinking and planning to develop meaningful solutions to combat these specific disparities and other priority issues

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: holistically through interagency leadership, partnership, and coordination, and through innovative governmental solutions grounded in law, policy, humanity, and the lived experiences and realities of LGBTQIA New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Over the course of its inaugural year, the Mayor's Office of LGBTQIA Affairs will take a phased and analytical approach to ensure that we meaningfully understand and prioritize all major issues facing LGBTQIA New Yorkers

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: with a focus on quality of life issues and baseline needs, including in the broad context of housing and subcontexts like housing insecurity and homelessness.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: The Mayor's Office of LGBTQIA Affairs will work to ensure that all New York City agencies are in compliance with non-discrimination law and protections and that they are utilizing best policies and practices to meet the needs of and their obligations to LGBTQIA New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: The Mayor's Office of LGBTQIA Affairs will ensure that at the executive level, across agencies and across government, we are delivering bold and innovative solutions and meaningful interventions to redress and eliminate disparities and further needed progress.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Finally, the Mayor's Office of LGBTQIA Affairs will work to ensure that we are a community-based and driven governmental office that provides meaningful leadership and responsive governance to the communities we serve.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Some of our current plans to implement this work include agency-wide regulatory and policy audits.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: to examine current policies and practices, assess what has been successful and where improvements can be made, and to work collaboratively towards better solutions and innovations to ensure that agencies are serving LGBTQI New Yorkers and addressing their unique needs.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Agency-wide programmatic evaluations to understand what city agencies have historically done and what they are currently doing in order to supplement, improve, and develop innovative governmental

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: interventions through programmatic and funding models grounded again in law, policy, data, community partnership, and centrally the shared goal of materially improving the lives of LGBTQI New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Substantive issue-based stakeholder convenings with our agency partners, inter- and intra-governmental partners, experts, advocates, and community-based organizations, as well as community members,

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: to ensure that the work of the Mayor's Office of LGBTQI Affairs is collaborative, holistic, responsive, and centered on the needs of the communities we serve.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: And finally, executive-level strategy, coordination, and innovation geared towards bold solutions and government intervention.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Interventions to eliminate the myriad of disparities facing LGBTQI New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: This includes combating, documenting federal hostility and the direct harms caused to LGBTQI New Yorkers, addressing historic and contemporary data and research needs and gaps that inform and drive our initiatives and goals,

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: and assessing and addressing governmental inefficiencies, policies, and practices that impact governmental operations and services.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: For example, communications, contracting, funding, and other operational elements to ensure that we are delivering better, more equitable, and more responsible services and models of governance.

chapters.summary: Brown concludes by affirming the office's expertise, vision, and dedication, and pledges the Mamdani administration's readiness to partner with City Council on addressing LGBTQIA housing instability.
chapters.title: Taylor Brown: Conclusion and commitment to partnership

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: The nexus of factors driving housing insecurity and homelessness disparities

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: for LGBTQIA New Yorkers are entrenched, intersectional, and complex, but they are not insurmountable.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: As the work of the Mayor's Office of LGBTQIA Affairs is developed and implemented, we want to assure committee members and City Council that we bring considerable substance of expertise and experience,

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: true vision and leadership, an unwavering dedication to the communities we serve in this city, and a deep commitment to transformational government and cross-government collaboration, cooperation, and

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: innovation to ensure that we are meeting the moment and establishing a lasting foundation for a more equitable, affordable, and accessible city for all.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Shared responsibilities and lasting solutions require us to address these interconnected challenges together.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: The Momdani administration, the Mayor's Office of LGBTQIA Affairs, and our agency partners stand by as ready and proactive partners.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: We look forward to new and continued partnership and collaboration with City Council on the issues before the committee today and those in the future.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Because LGBTQIA New Yorkers, like all New Yorkers, deserve the opportunity to live and thrive with safety, dignity, and stability.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify, and I'll welcome the committee's questions.

chapters.summary: DSS Commissioner Erin Dalton introduces joined officials and describes DSS's commitment to culturally competent services, recognizing unique experiences of LGBTQIA and TGNC communities seeking assistance.
chapters.title: Erin Dalton: Introduction and DSS commitment to vulnerable populations

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: My name is Erin Dalton and I serve as the Commissioner for New York City Department of Social Services and my pronouns are she and her.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I would like to thank Chair Hudson, Chair Farias, and the members of the Committee on General Welfare, the members of the Committee on Women and Gender Equity for convening today's hearing on housing instability among LGBTQIA individuals in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I am joined today by John Rojas, HRA Chief Special Services Officer, Rima Rivera, HRA Deputy Commissioner for Domestic Violence Services, Andrea Reed,

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: HRA Executive Deputy Commissioner for Housing Services, and Sonia Russell, DHS Interim Deputy Commissioner for Adult Services and Shelter Operations.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Serving vulnerable populations is an integral part of our work at DSS.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Through our work at the Human Resources Administration, or HRA, and the Department of Homeless Services, or DHS, we understand that our clients are reaching out for assistance precisely because they are in an acutely vulnerable moment.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: As stewards of critical programs and resources, it is incumbent upon us to recognize that one size does not fit all.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: That is why recognizing the unique experiences of lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer, questioning, intersex, and asexual, LGBTQIA, transgender, and gender nonconforming TGNC communities is so important to our work.

chapters.summary: Dalton details DSS's mandatory LGBTQIA inclusion training for all staff within 90 days of hire, biennial refreshers, and specialized placement procedure training for TGNC clients.
chapters.title: Erin Dalton: Cultural competence and staff training requirements

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: To that end, DSS is committed to ensuring our services are culturally competent and welcoming.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: At DSS, we are cognizant of the scale of the challenge in addressing housing security.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: for LGBTQIA and TGNC communities.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Unique drivers for homelessness include familial rejection due to sexual orientation or gender expression and unlawful housing and employment discrimination.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: An intersectional lens informs this conversation and our policies by bringing to the fore the way social identities interact with these communities.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Systemic inequalities and discrimination can compound the challenges members of these communities face, and we cannot flatten the diversity experiences within the LGBTQIA and TGNC communities.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: For example, the many impacts of age, race, ethnicity, language, disability, class, documentation status, education, mental health, and more.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Providing culturally competent services includes recognizing the reality of our clients'experiences at those intersections of identities, strengthening our partnerships with community-based organizations, and ongoing engagement to gather feedback and improve services.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Building cultural competence begins with the foundation of setting expectations and training staff to meet those expectations.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: DSS Office of LGBTQIA Affairs works to identify and address systemic barriers affecting clients across DSS, HRA, DHS programs.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: This work includes improving service delivery, strengthening internal and external partnerships, and responding directly to client concerns related to access, discrimination, or safety.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: All DSS, DHS, and HRA staff are required to attend an LGBTQIA inclusion training.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: In addition to the mandatory

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: One-time training.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Employees are also required to attend refresher trainings every two years.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Providers and their staff are required to comply with New York City human rights law and citywide equal employment opportunity policy.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Providers may also require that staff attend trainings beyond what's required by city law and policy, including trainings focused on the unique needs of TGNC clients.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: One such training available to city and provider staff is the placement procedure training for transgender and gender nonconforming clients, which focuses on creating a safe, affirming system for clients who self-identify as TGNC

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: and raises awareness about more gender-affirming placement options for TGNC individuals that are seeking shelter.

chapters.summary: Dalton acknowledges that voluntary gender identity and sexual orientation surveys at intake yield low participation, resulting in significant undercounting of LGBTQIA and TGNC clients across DSS systems.
chapters.title: Erin Dalton: Data collection limitations and voluntary screening

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: There are limitations we must acknowledge, especially as it relates to data collection.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: DHS seeks to affirm each individual's gender identity and ensure that they are appropriately placed by giving clients the option to self-identify their gender through a voluntary gender identity screening, which occurs at intake.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Clients who self-identify as TGNC are then given their gender-affirming placement options.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: DHS clients are also offered voluntary sexual orientation and gender identity survey at intake.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: However, because this is voluntary and not linked to a person's case or placement, it means participation is low and our data on TG...

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: LGBTQIA and TGNC clients is limited.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: On the HRA side of the house, sexual orientation and gender identity data is voluntary and

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: offered for clients enrolled in cash assistance, SNAP, and IDNYC benefits programs, as well as those seeking services for domestic violence per local law 130.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: We acknowledge our data represents an undercount of LGBTQIA and TGNC clients.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Thus, the data we have significantly under-represents the actual number of individuals receiving services across

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: the DV and DHS shelter system.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: The voluntary data we collect is a factor that assists DSS in determining programmatic capacity needs and potential policy adjustments that could be made to better address the needs of the population.

chapters.summary: Dalton describes LGBTQIA-affirming requirements at all DHS sites, plus specialized shelters including ACE's Place (150 TGNC beds), Marsha's House (80 LGBTQIA beds), and 42 additional TGNC beds across four boroughs.
chapters.title: Erin Dalton: Affirming environments and specialized shelters

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: All DHS sites and provider locations are required by contract to be LGBTQIA affirming environments.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Our goal is to create safe environments where LGBTQIA individuals are treated with the same degree of respect and dignity as all other identities.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Affirming environments include visible signs in client and staff areas to make clear LGBTQIA people are welcome and valued parts of the community.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: For example, DSS ally cards, welcome palm cards, LGBTQIA themed posters.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: In addition to the responsibility of all shelter staff to be LGBTQIA affirming environments, DHS also has specialized shelters and beds that are exclusively dedicated to serving the LGBTQIA and TGNC clients.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: DHS goal and current practice is for individuals who self-report TGNC to receive expedited placement to a TGNC designated bed if that's what they choose.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: This place serves as the nation's first TGNC-specific shelter site that can accommodate 150 residents.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Similarly, Marsha's house focuses on serving the broader spectrum of LGBTQIA residents, accommodating 80 residents overall.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: In addition, there are 42 beds at six shelters focused on serving TGNC clients at shelters in the Bronx, Brooklyn, Manhattan, and Queens.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Regarding HRA programs, gender identity is considered by HASA when making emergency housing placements and flagged by client's preference.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Domestic violence services supports to both residential and non-residential services that are available to LGBTQIA and TGNC survivors.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Our OCFS licensed

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: DV shelters and tier two shelters within the DHS system are required to serve all survivors who meet domestic violence eligibility criteria, regardless of sexual orientation, gender identity or expression.

chapters.summary: Dalton outlines domestic violence shelter services for LGBTQIA survivors, including a contract with the LGBT Center for non-residential counseling, case management, and advocacy without requiring shelter entry.
chapters.title: Erin Dalton: DV services and community partnerships

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: So LGBTQIA and TGNC survivors have full access to residential services when DV shelter is the safest option.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: In addition, we fund non-residential supportive services in the community, including services specifically dedicated to LGBTQIA survivors through a contract with the Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Transgender, Queer Center, known as the Center.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Through this partnership, clients can access culturally competent counseling, safety planning, case management, advocacy, support groups, and referrals without needing to enter shelter, ensuring there are

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: affirming options for both inside and outside the residential system.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: The agency regularly checks in with advocates and clients on ways to improve our service model and to ensure that there are mechanisms and systems in place for LGBTQIA plus individuals to share their feedback on agency policy, services, and programs.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: To that end, both clients and advocates serve on advisory boards.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: For example, the LGBTQIA plus work group, HASA community advisory board, and the shelter accessibility advisory board.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: We also receive feedback and complaints directly from clients through 311, the Office of the Ombudsman, the DSS Office of Equal Employment Opportunities, or the Office of

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: LGBTQIA plus affairs within DSS or any other avenues in which we receive feedback.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Our goal is to ensure this population has ample options to offer that feedback.

chapters.summary: Dalton highlights the forthcoming TGNC advisory group of 6-8 clients with homelessness experience, and the Shelter Accessibility Board's recommendations including safety escorts and transportation considerations for TGNC clients.
chapters.title: Erin Dalton: TGNC advisory group and Shelter Accessibility Board

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I want to take this opportunity to spotlight our forthcoming TGNC advisory group.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: It is a group that has been several years in the making.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: This client advisory group aims to engage community-based expertise, bring insights around what is working and what is not working, and what measures we can take to improve programs, services, and the experiences of TGNC individuals in the shelter system.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: This advisory group will include six to eight clients who have experience with homelessness, and we aim for this advisory group to begin meeting later this year.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I also want to highlight the work of our Shelter Accessibility Board here.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: This nine-member board meets quarterly and is the result of Local Law 23 of 2023.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: The board issued its first suite of recommendations in January.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Those initial recommendations centered on acts

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: access support, workforce and staffing, and critical amenities.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: The board also focused on inclusivity, especially the safety needs of the TGNC population.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: The board recommended providers assess and mitigate client safety concerns in their community for individuals and their independent living plan.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: For example, the advisory board recommended shelter staff

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: offer escorts to the bus and train.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: As a result of these recommendations, the TGNC shelter is now offering these escorts.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: The board also recommended that DHS take into account and consideration accessibility of transportation when making shelter placement determinations.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: We are taking a close look at how we can move forward with the board's recommendations and remain committed to an approach to service provision that ensures client safety and breaks down obstacles to housing and security.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: To provide an example on the point of breaking down barriers, we know that identification document mismatch can create barriers for our transgender clients.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And our DSS, HRA, DHS systems providers and staff, in accordance with New York City law, use a person's preferred name even if they have not legally changed it.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: External entities, credit checks, proof of employment, or lease documents present challenges for transgender people seeking housing.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: In alignment with our commitment to helping clients find the support they need, staff are expected to work with TGNC clients to facilitate their road to independent living, including referrals for legal assistance with name changes.

chapters.summary: Dalton describes DSS community outreach connecting LGBTQIA individuals to resources through organizations like Ali Forney and Bronx Legal Services, and concludes by affirming the Mamdani administration's commitment to LGBTQIA dignity.
chapters.title: Erin Dalton: Community outreach and conclusion

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Our Office of Community Outreach, alongside our Office of LGBTQIA Affairs, serves as a crucial hub in connecting LGBTQIA.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: UIA Plus and TGNC communities.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Their work includes providing resources to community organizations like the Center, Ali Forney, and Bronx Legal Services who work with DSS, HRA, DHS clients and assisting them to navigate benefits access.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Our work includes providing dedicated trainings on benefits to community providers, sharing monthly updates through a newsletter to community providers, and coordinating access for more than 450 community-based organizations in total using the Access HRA provider portal.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: We also participate in panels, school and community events, shelter house meetings, and LGBTQIA plus support groups.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I would like to take this opportunity to share what Mayor Mamdani has said.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: New York City is proud of its LGBTQIA plus community.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: We refuse to deny health care safety or dignity to anyone on the basis of their identity.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: DSS will continue to uphold our commitment to ensuring all of our services for the LGBTQIA plus culturally competent and welcoming.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I will conclude by expressing my gratitude to my colleagues at DSS and across government, as well as countless human services providers, advocates, and community members, uplifting the dignity of our LGBTQIA plus and TGNC family, friends, and neighbors.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: We appreciate this opportunity to testify today, to say happy pride in this forum, and we welcome your questions.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: DYCD Associate Commissioner Wanda Ascherl introduces the agency's runaway and homeless youth services, noting LGBTQ+ youth face disproportionate risks of housing instability, exploitation, and adverse health outcomes.
chapters.title: Wanda Ascherl: Introduction and DYCD recognition of LGBTQ+ youth risks

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Sessing.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Good.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Good morning, Chairs Hudson and Farias, and members of the Committees on General Welfare, Children and Youth, and Women and Gender Equity.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: I am Associate Commissioner Wanda Asherl, pronouns she and her, at the New York City Department of Youth and Community Development.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: I am joined today by Tracy Thorne, pronouns she and her, Senior Director of Runaway and Homeless Youth Services.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: On behalf of Commissioner Escamilla-Davies, we thank you for the opportunity to discuss how DYCD addresses housing instability among LGBTQI plus individuals.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: We also wish a happy Pride Month to LGBTQ plus New Yorkers and allies.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: DYCD recognizes that LGBTQ plus youth may face discrimination and harassment that puts them at disproportionate risks of housing instability, exploitation, and adverse health and mental health outcomes.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Access to trusted adults, affirming environments, and high-quality services is critical to ensuring their safety, well-being, and long-term success.

chapters.summary: Ascherl details DYCD's 863 residential beds for youth ages 16-24, including 160 beds for young adults 21-24, with 100 new beds added this fiscal year under Chair Stevens's leadership.
chapters.title: Wanda Ascherl: RHY programs and bed capacity

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Today, with our dedicated provider network, advocates, and the City Council, DYCD has built a comprehensive continuum of services

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: designed to meet the diverse needs of runaway and homeless youth ages 16 through 24.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Our Runaway and Homeless Youth, RHY,

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Programs provide holistic, youth-centered services that help young people achieve stability, independence, and opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Through residential services, drop-in centers, and street outreach programs, DYCD and its providers serve more than 40,000 young people annually.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Residential services include crisis services programs and transitional independent living support programs.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Across the city, residential programs serving youth ages 16 through 24, providing a total of 863 beds.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: For homeless young adults ages 21 to 24, DYCD funds six programs with a total of 160 beds, 50 of which are scheduled to open in the next six weeks.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: We are especially pleased that in partnership with the council and under the leadership of Chair Stevens,

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: This capacity was expanded by 100 beds for homeless young adults in this fiscal year, strengthening our ability to meet growing needs.

chapters.summary: Ascherl describes eight drop-in centers with 24/7 borough coverage offering food, clothing, counseling, and referrals, plus street outreach teams engaging youth at known congregation sites six days per week.
chapters.title: Wanda Ascherl: Drop-in centers and street outreach

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: DYCD's eight contracted drop-in centers are a safe place for LGBTQ plus youth and other young people to access essential resources such as food,

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: clothing, and hygiene supplies, alongside a wide range of supportive services, including counseling, educational and health workshops,

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: recreational programming, and referrals.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: To additional services and shelter when needed.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Specialized staff, including housing navigators, financial coaches, peer navigators, and mental health clinicians work closely with young and young adults to help them identify goals

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: and take meaningful steps toward long-term stability and self-sufficiency.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: To ensure accessibility, at least one drop-in center in every borough operates 24 hours a day, seven days a week.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Our street outreach programs engage young people where they are, focusing on locations where runaway and homeless youth are known to congregate.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Outreach teams provide immediate support, information, safety planning, and connections to housing and other critical services.

chapters.summary: Ascherl outlines individualized service plans, family reunification support, and professional development for providers on gender-affirming interactions, highlighting mission-oriented organizations like Ali Forney Center.
chapters.title: Wanda Ascherl: Case management and LGBTQ+ affirming providers

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: DYCD-funded RHY providers help young people navigate a wide range of challenges through individualized counseling, case management, and service referrals.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: From intake assessments through ongoing case management, providers work to identify each young person's strengths, needs, and goals while connecting them to services that support their overall well-being and including

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: in specialized mental health care when appropriate.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: All our RHY providers are trained for and welcoming to LGBTQ plus youth, including mission oriented organizations like Ali Forney Center, all of which have particular particular expertise.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: and focus on serving LGBTQ plus young people experiencing homelessness.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: This includes professional development regarding sexual orientation, gender identity, and gender expression, gender affirming interactions that foster respect and appreciation, physical, safe,

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: physically safe and culturally positive environments, and swift response to issues of bias and discrimination.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: In RHI programs, counselors work collaboratively with youth to develop individualized service plans that establish both short and long-term goals.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Through direct services and community partnerships, youth can access a broad range of supports, including physical and mental health services, educational and career

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: development opportunities, substance use prevention services, violence intervention and prevention counseling, and housing assistance.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: When appropriate, providers also support family reunification efforts or help youth transition to longer-term housing and supportive programs.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Thank you again for the opportunity to discuss the EYCD's efforts to address housing instability among LGBTQ plus youth.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: We remain committed to working alongside the council, providers, advocates, and young people with lived experience to strengthen services and expand opportunities for the youth we serve.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: We are pleased to answer any questions you may have.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you so much for all of you for such thoughtful testimony.

chapters.summary: Farías asks Brown about specific housing supports, budgetary needs, interagency coordination with DSS and DYCD, and whether the office will offer LGBTQ+ trainings. Brown says planning is ongoing, budget negotiations continue with OMB, and trainings will be assessed against existing offerings.
chapters.title: Farías questions Brown on housing supports, budget, and trainings

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I would like to start off with some questions for the Office of LGBTQ Affairs.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: What supports will be offered by the office around housing for LGBTQ plus individuals?

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: So that is certainly still within our planning.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: As you know, we're a new office.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: We're about two months in.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: We're also in the midst of budget season.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: And so while our strategic planning has been ongoing, I want to stress that a big part of it will be based in agency partnerships.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: And again, making sure they are abiding by the law, making sure that they have the best policies and practices.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: But most importantly, I think what I have brought to this office is innovation.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: And to me, that means doing more.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: I think that's what's required.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And so some of the agencies you're planning to coordinate with are like DSS, HRA, DYCD around housing services for LGBTQ+.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: As I understand the mandates for this office, as informed by the mayor's agenda, we reach across all agencies in New York City and all agencies that touch the lives of LGBTQI New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Do you think there's any necessary budgetary items that your office will require in order to accomplish some of this?

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: So those negotiations are ongoing, and we have contacts with OMB.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: We're housed within the Mayor's Office of Equity and Racial Justice, and so our budget is also tied to that as well.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: And so right now I would defer to my OMB colleagues and contacts.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: I remain hopeful.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Okay, we'll bother them.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: We got it.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Are you planning on offering any specific trainings on serving LGBTQ plus individuals seeking housing services from the office itself?

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: You know, I think one thing that I'm careful of in coming into this position is not, you know, being unnecessarily repetitive.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: And so I want to make sure that I have a good assessment of what's available now.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: There are trainings that are offered through CCHR.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Obviously, there are mandated trainings that are required by law.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: There's trainings provided by DSS, as Commissioner Dalton mentioned.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: I inherited ongoing work in compliance with Local Law 71 and 73, which also tie into rights and resources, and also as well as different kinds of campaigns, which include trainings focused on

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: rights and resources for TGNC and MB New Yorkers, as well as the unique needs of immigrant LGBTQI New Yorkers as well.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: And so I certainly have the expertise and certainly can make that available.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: And I think, again, I think it just takes an assessment of what's available, but would be happy to offer it.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: That's great.

chapters.summary: Farías asks about maintaining the Unity Project's housing resource guide, community outreach campaigns, and the office's budget allocation. Brown commits to keeping the guide updated, notes no active outreach campaigns yet, and describes inherited funding including a $400,000 DOHMH allocation.
chapters.title: Farías questions Brown on Unity Project guide, outreach, and budget allocation

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: The New York City Unity Project has maintained a guide of organizations working to support housing and shelter services for LGBTQ plus individuals in the past.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: As the new office is set to absorb the Unity Project, will you keep the guide up to date?

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: And not just in the housing space, but across all areas of life.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And are there any thoughts on planning or offering any community outreach or education campaigns around the issue itself?

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Right now, in this moment, I cannot say that we have any active.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: plans, but I think, again, that is something that we are considering.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: I think from my own personal experience and background, when people are informed of their rights and are aware of their rights, that is how they're best enforced and people are best made to abide by them.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: So certainly something we will consider.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And just my last question for us as chairs, do you have an allocation for your budget already set?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: For the office?

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: So that terminology is new to me, new to government.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: So as I understand it, we do have an allocation for staffing lines.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: We do have an allocation for some programmatic work, which was one of the primary functions of the Unity project.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: And again, I think that my understanding is those exact amounts are the negotiations are ongoing.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Okay, do you know the previous amount?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: If there were any towards the services that are under your bucket now?

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: So the way that the Unity Project worked primarily was through budget allocations that lied in other agencies.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: For example, I'm aware of a $400,000 fund that lies within DOHMH.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: And so this would be specifically tied to sort of health care program and funding.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: There were some one-time grant opportunities, including the Transgender Emergency Fund, the Unity grants.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: But from my general understanding of the 10 years of Unity Project sort of funding model is that none of them were sort of, I think the $400,000 one is the only one that's sort of every year.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Good for us to know.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you so much for answering my questions.

chapters.summary: Farías asks how many LGBTQ youth are served and what percentage they represent. Ascherl reports 700 LGBTQIA+ youth in residential programs and 564 at drop-in centers, comprising 38% of program enrollees and 42% of drop-in center participants.
chapters.title: Farías questions DYCD on LGBTQ youth served and percentages

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'm going to move over to DYCD.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: How many LGBTQ youth experiencing homelessness are currently being served through DYCD-funded programs?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Youth currently are not required to disclose gender identity or sexual orientation with their RHI programs.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: But today, as of May 2026, 700 youth who reported as identifying as LGBTQIA plus were entered into our residential programs.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: And then we had 564 young people that reported as identifying LGBTQIA plus started receiving case management and services at the drop-in centers.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And what percentage of youth in DYCD-funded runaway and homeless youth programs identify as LGBTQ plus?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: We have 38% of the young people have been entered into our programs and identifying as LGBTQIA.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: And then we have 35% of the young people who entered our residential programs that reported the same.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: And then 42% were in our drop-in centers.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Farías asks how DYCD ensures LGBTQ youth know about services. Ascherl describes word-of-mouth referrals, social media presence, Discover DYCD, palm cards, and street outreach vans as key outreach channels.
chapters.title: Farías questions DYCD on outreach and service access for LGBTQ youth

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And how do you folks at TYCD ensure that the LGBTQs know about the available services and can access them safely?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Obviously, like the drop-in centers are a great example where there's 24-hour care and they can go in at any point.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: But other than services like the drop-in centers, how are we looking at making those available and the outreach?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Yeah, I think one of the things that

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: works and we've seen it historically and I think it works with any programs like word of mouth I think young people come you know talk to each other and they and they

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: and they hear about the great things that are happening and so that's been really really helpful especially for all LGBTQIA young population they we rely on refer

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: We rely on referrals.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: DYCD also has a strong social media presence and Instagram and Facebook, Twitter.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: We also have Discover DYCD where all the services are.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Are available and accessible and people can inquire about these programs.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: And then we have the palm cards, which we actually brought extras so we can distribute some of those.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: And that's also available.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: And then we also have the street outreach piece where we have six days per week.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Assisting youth in need, and then, as you mentioned, our drop-in centers, and then the street outreach vans, as I mentioned.

chapters.summary: Farías asks how outreach teams determine where to go. Tracy Thorne explains Streetworks operates north and south vans, with weekly locations based on field observations and drop-in center feedback, covering parks, subways, and other youth congregation sites.
chapters.title: Farías questions DYCD on street outreach team locations

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: In terms of the street outreach team, how do you folks determine where the outreach team needs to go?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Is it data aggregated?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Is it, again, a word-of-mouth situation where we're hearing from folks that are already receiving services that there's a big need in a community?

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: The street outreach is run by Streetworks, a Safe Horizon program, and they determine every week they send out a list of where they're going to be.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: There's a north and a south van, and it's based on what they see when they're out there.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: It's based on what they're hearing from young people at the drop-in centers, and so it changes.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: Their locations change as needed, but they cover parks.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: subways, and any other places that they're hearing that young people are.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And is it they're providing services on site, like immediate care that's needed or a resource that's needed, but also helping bring folks into a drop-in center?

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: That's correct.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: That's absolutely correct.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Farías asks about long-term housing outcomes for LGBTQ youth. Ascherl reports 15% returned home, 20% found other residences, 12% transitioned to independent living, and 42% received crisis or supportive services, with five-year trend data to follow up.
chapters.title: Farías questions DYCD on long-term housing outcomes data

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Can you folks describe any long-term housing outcomes, if we have that data, for LGBTQ

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: youth who participate in the DYCD-funded programs, and if we know how many LGBTQ youth successfully transition into permanent housing each year for the last five years or three years?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: So according to the most recent data concerning long-term housing, we have 15% of our young people were returned home.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: And 20% had established other residents, whether it was through friends or relatives.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: We had 12% that transitioned into independent.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: living and then we also we had 42% that received a variety of services including crisis services foster home care DHS shelters and supportive housing

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: and then there were some that were unknown for us and then

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: we have a more detailed report that we can make available.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: We don't have the information readily available for like the five-year trend, and we're going to look into that and make that available for you.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: That would be extremely helpful, obviously, as we're trying to see where trends are and where more resources need to go.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Every budget fight requires this kind of data to look into, so that would be really helpful.

chapters.summary: Farías asks whether DYCD tracks youth after placement to ensure stability beyond couch-surfing. Ascherl describes continuous counseling and support connections, though specific post-placement duration tracking is not detailed.
chapters.title: Farías questions DYCD on post-placement tracking

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: In terms of the percentages for folks, you know,

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: quote unquote, returned home and either finding placement with friends or family?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Are we looking at ensuring that they have like an individual room?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: We're not making sure folks are maybe transitioning into someone's home, but like on a couch for six months.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Like are we tracking post one year, post two years?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: How long are we also tracking the folks that are coming in for services?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: I can start.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: And then you'll correct me if I'm wrong.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: I'll take the lead.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: I'll start and then I'll tap in Tracy if I'm missing something.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: She'll keep me honest.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: I will say that our young people have continuous...

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Counseling and support.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: services.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: And so our providers ensure that there's a touch point and there's a continuous connection to make sure that the young people are receiving services, additional supportive services, whether it's employment or education.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: And so they try to continue to make those connections even while they're in a

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: in a place, in a house, in a home.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Is that correct?

chapters.summary: Farías asks what family reunification programs look like and whether they're proactive. Ascherl and Thorne explain services are youth-driven, with providers offering counseling and support to both youth and families when reunification is desired.
chapters.title: Farías questions DYCD on family reunification programs

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And then I hear, or in the testimony, there was a program or initiative for preventing family rejection or reducing or reunifying.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I think the language is reunification.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: What are some of those programs or initiatives?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Like, what do they look like?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: How do we get to it before it occurs?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Are we reactive in a way in terms of the reunification back into the home?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: So our drop-in centers, they offer supportive services for our young people that are going toward becoming homeless.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: And so some of the services includes counseling and referrals to health care, education, and employment opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: When LGBTQ youth are served in our programs, there is a unique opportunity to create a safe and protective space.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: They build relationships with the young people that are there.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: The programs are...

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: offer an opportunity for you to create a caring communities.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: And then because the programs are youth-centered, services, including family unification, are driven by young people's youth voice.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: So they pretty much let the provider and the counselor know whether or not they want to reunite with their families.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: And then they receive, if they don't, they receive ongoing support and counseling.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
