utterances.text: If they do, the family receives support services as well as, so the whole family as well as individuals.

chapters.summary: Farías asks whether DYCD hears reasons for family rejection and uses that to shape services, citing machismo and patriarchal systems. Thorne confirms family rejection is a top reason youth seek services and that support is case-by-case and youth-driven.
chapters.title: Farías questions DYCD on family disconnect and patriarchal systems

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Are we hearing from young people what the reasoning is for either the disconnect at home or getting kicked out of their home from their family?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Are we using any of that information to kind of dictate the type of, whether it's...

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Therapy sessions or what the reunification process looks like.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Like I imagine patriarchal systems are something that are heavy on why some folks have to leave their household or religious and cultural issues.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'm just wondering how are we taking those lived experiences and then responding

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: both as a city, I think beyond just this moment, we really should be looking at how to deal with

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: machismo and patriarchal systems in this way, but also just like how are we empowering the young people in this position and going back to their family or living independently?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Like are those conversations

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: A way that part of how we're determining services to young people.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: Yeah, I think when Wanda mentioned that the young people drive the services that they require, that's how we're ensuring that we're offering young people

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: what they need to feel supported, to move along in their journey toward

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: self-sufficiency and feeling and increasing their resilience.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: So it's a case-by-case basis, but we know that from our intakes that family rejection and family discord is one of the number one reasons that young people are coming to us for services, and we're here for them.

chapters.summary: Farías asks how DYCD ensures LGBTQ+ youth referred to DHS are placed in gender-affirming shelters. Thorne describes close coordination with DHS intake staff and successful placements at Marsha's House, ACE's Place, and other affirming sites.
chapters.title: Farías questions DYCD on streamlined referral to DHS for LGBTQ+ youth

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: When youth age out of the runaway and homeless youth shelters, there's a process by which they can be referred directly to DHS.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: According to the most recent streamlined referral process report from the period of January 1 through June 30th of 2025, 40 young people were referred to DHS.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: How does DYCD ensure that LGBTQ plus youth who are referred to DHS get placed within a shelter which matches their gender identity or into a specialized LGBTQ plus bed should that be their preference?

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: The intake staff at DHS are our partners.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: We're in close contact with the DHS.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: contact almost every day and they do a great job making sure that young people are placed in programs that are affirming and meet their needs.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: We have a lot of great placements with Marsha's House, ACES Place and other sites.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: And if there is a need for further

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: clarification if maybe there was something that wasn't listed on the streamlined referral form.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: DHS is really responsive to making sure that people are placed in the right locations.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And as we've already spoken about a bit, young people enter

chapters.summary: Farías asks about entry pathway breakdowns and why 36% of 592 unplaced youth identified as LGBTQ. Thorne cites lack of bed availability as the primary reason for non-placement and promises to follow up on pathway data.
chapters.title: Farías questions DYCD on entry pathways and LL79 placement gaps

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: crisis shelters by referral, walking in themselves, or walking into a drop-in center, or even via the street outreach team.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Do you folks have an idea of what percent of young people enter shelters via each of these pathways?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Do we have that breakdown of whether it's street team, whether it's the referral or walking in on their own?

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: We could get back to you about that.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And my last question before I kick it over to Chair Hudson.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: According to Local Law 79 report on the Runaway and Homeless Youth Service Access Report for the period July through December of 2025,

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: of the 592 youth not placed, 36% identified as LGBTQ.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Do you know the reason for the lack of the placements?

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: Generally, the reason for the lack of placements is lack of bed availability.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Sorry?

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: Lack of bed availability.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Bed availability.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Thank you so much for answering my questions.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: I'll kick it over to Council Member Hudson.

chapters.summary: Hudson asks who provides family mediation and reunification services. Ascherl confirms providers deliver services directly, naming Ali Forney Center and Rising Ground among them.
chapters.title: Hudson questions DYCD on family mediation providers

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I did have one follow-up question from Chair Farias about the support services that are provided for families.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Who provides those services?

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Are those provided by DYCD or do you subcontract to other?

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: organizations.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: The CB, the...

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Around family mediation and reunification?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Our providers provide those services directly.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: So who are some of those providers?

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Ali Forney, off the top of my head, I can't remember all of them.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: Rising Ground.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Wanda Ascherl
utterances.text: To name a few.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay, thanks.

chapters.summary: Hudson asks how DSS coordinates with other agencies. Dalton describes collaboration with HPD, NYCHA, and aging services, noting good advocacy from mayoral agencies to ensure LGBTQ+ clients access needed resources.
chapters.title: Hudson questions DSS on inter-agency coordination for LGBTQ+ housing

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I'm going to ask questions of DSS.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: So how does DSS coordinate with other city agencies to meet the needs of homeless and housing insecure LGBTQ plus individuals?

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And as you know, Chair Hudson and others probably know, I've been here just a couple of months, and so it's a great opportunity for me to learn a little bit more, deepen my understanding of the work we do in this area.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: This one I can take, but I will probably rely on my colleagues here, probably especially Deputy Commissioner Russell for detailed questions.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: But yes, when it comes to...

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Addressing the issues of housing insecurity for this population.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: We certainly work across the team who is here on the dais.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: We also work with our partners in housing like HPD and NYCHA.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And so in addition for special populations like older adults, we work with our partners in aging and so on.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: So I think there's a good amount of collaboration and I think a good amount of advocacy from the mayor's agencies and departments to ensure that the clients that they're working with are treated well and get the access to the resources they need in our systems.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Hudson asks about intake for LGBTQ+ veterans seeking shelter. Sonia Russell describes the intake process including voluntary gender ID screening, self-identification options, and accommodations for both veteran and TGNC status.
chapters.title: Hudson questions DSS on Veteran Services coordination for LGBTQ+ veterans

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: How does DSS coordinate with the Department for Veteran Services to ensure that LGBTQ plus veterans seeking shelter are housed appropriately?

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And can you share what that intake process looks like?

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: So DHS has intake centers that serves those that are seeking temporary housing assistance throughout the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: We have intake centers for families with children.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: We have those for adult families, as well as single men and single women.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: And it's at the intake center where we ask a series of questions for those that are seeking temporary housing to better assist them based on their circumstances and needs that brought them into the system.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: We also offer a...

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: Voluntary gender ID screener for those who, while we don't ask for their specific sexual orientation, we do have this voluntary screener.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: And depending on how they, if they choose to either complete the screener or if they prefer to self-identify.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: So for those individuals that prefer to self-identify as either being part of the LGBTQI or TGNC,

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: in addition to if they articulate that they are a veteran, we will work with them to provide accommodation that best suits their needs as LGBTQI, TGNC, and or a veteran.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: Or if they just choose to go into a TGNC program, we will definitely offer them.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: those accommodations as well.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Hudson asks about shelter placement for LGBTQ+ older adults. Russell describes shelters for 55+ and 60+ populations, voluntary gender ID screening, and reasonable accommodation processes for mobility and accessibility needs.
chapters.title: Hudson questions DSS on NYC Aging coordination for LGBTQ+ older adults

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And then the same question with regards to NYC aging and LGBTQIA plus older adults seeking shelter and whether or not they're housed appropriately.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: Thank you again.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: So it's similar to the intake process that we offer for those who are veterans.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: So we do have shelters that are specific to our mature unhoused population, 55 plus or 60 plus who are unhoused.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: And again, while we don't ask for their sexual orientation, they can complete the voluntary ID screener and or self-identify.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: And for those individuals who have a mobility and or accessibility needs, the agency does offer what's called a reasonable accommodation process.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: And this is for those individuals where the accommodation is not apparent and or obvious.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: So if someone has specific needs, they will complete this reasonable accommodation, they will go through the process, and we will place them into facilities that's specific to their mobility and accommodation needs.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

chapters.summary: Hudson presses on how proactively staff offer the gender identity screener, comparing it to accessibility accommodations. Russell confirms all clients are asked to complete the screener and for preferred names, with transfers available if identity is disclosed later.
chapters.title: Hudson questions DSS on proactive gender identity screening at intake

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And then Chair Fideas and I were discussing, I mean, we totally understand, obviously, and particularly as legislators, the legal limitations that exist around identification.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: But we're curious to know how...

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: aggressively, for lack of a better word,

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: you all are offering the opportunity for people to, you know, identify a particular way.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Because if it's something where somebody mentions it in a passing conversation, as opposed to

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: a staff and the front lines, you know, saying, if you happen to identify this particular way, we have resources and services available to you to address specific needs.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: So we're just trying to understand how aggressive.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Those options are being provided.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And just as an example, for an accessibility need or a disability, I'm sure we're pretty aggressively offering, if you have

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: a need or a disability that is invisible to us, we want you to know that, are we doing the same for LGBTQIA plus folks?

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: That are coming through the system?

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: Thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: So while we don't want to necessarily push or force individuals to identify as such, again, we do ask if they want to complete the voluntary gender ID screener.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: So you're asking everybody that?

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: And we're also asking for their preferred name.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: So some individuals may come in as Sonia, but I may say my preferred name is Samuel.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: And it's that point that we will ask whether or not they want to voluntarily complete the screener.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: And if not, if they say, you know, well, I identify this.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: and I wish to be placed in a shelter that, you know, based on how I identify, then we will accommodate that.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: But again, we want to be sensitive and not overly aggressive with individuals that come into our shelter system and allow them to identify how they wish to do so.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: And they may choose not to identify at intake, but perhaps they identify once they are placed in their shelter.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: And again, they're offered the opportunity to say, you know what, I'd rather be placed in a shelter, you know, again, based on how I self-identify.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: And which we will allow them to transfer to that specific shelter as well.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I probably should have used proactively rather than aggressively.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I didn't mean to imply you should aggressively ask people.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: But the point is, and I just want to clarify, are you actually asking everyone during intake if they'd like to complete the form?

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Or is it only when somebody has perhaps?

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Shared some bit of information relevant that maybe then they're being offered the opportunity to complete that form or assessment.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: So I would say it's both.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: We are required to ask them, but again, if someone offers information, then the staff will definitely say, you know, please, you know, you should fill the questionnaire, the gender screener.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

chapters.summary: Hudson asks what training street outreach workers receive for engaging LGBTQ+ individuals. Dalton describes mandatory LGBTQIA+ training within 90 days and biennial refreshers, plus specialized TGNC placement training, and emphasizes the need for individualized solutions.
chapters.title: Hudson questions DSS on outreach worker training for LGBTQ+ street homeless

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: DSS is dedicated outreach to street homeless individuals.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: What training do outreach workers receive in relation to engaging with LGBTQ plus individuals who are street homeless, especially in relation to support?

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Their transition into shelters.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: All DHS staff as well as DHS contracted providers are required to have an LGBTQIA plus training within 90 days of start date and with every two years thereafter.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: So there is required training.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: In addition, our providers may offer additional support and training.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Just one example of training that's available to city and provider staff is the placement procedure training for transgender and gender nonconforming clients.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: So that's one additional opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And when it comes to coordinating...

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: placement in the shelter system.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I think particularly for folks who are on the street, unhoused, we really do need to be at least aspiring to creative individualized solutions for those folks.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: We know our shelter system works pretty well for the vast majority of people, but for people who are on the streets today, we want to make sure we're offering those individualized solutions.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: We do train our staff and our providers when new

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: opportunities for housing come on board.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And so we're doing that kind of work, but also I'm particularly interested in understanding how individualized and creative we can be for people who are on the street.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Farías asks about tracking incidents. Dalton provides FY26 data through June 16: 61 gender discrimination reports, 24 TGNC discrimination, 5 TGNC harassment, and 2 TGNC retaliation across DHS and HRA, totaling 92 reports via the IQ system.
chapters.title: Farías questions DSS on tracking harassment and violence incidents

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And do you all track incidents of harassment, bullying, or violence towards LGBTQ plus shelter residents?

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: We do.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And I can provide that data for the last year if that's helpful.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Yeah, that'd be great.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And I'm providing it in the way that it is tracked in our systems, which may not correspond exactly to the question.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: So this is for July 1, 2025 through June 16, 2026, so not through the full year.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And so in terms of discrimination type for gender discrimination across DHS shelters and HRA shelters, the count is 61.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I can break that down further if you'd like.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: If you've got it further.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I've got it.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I've got it.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: So for gender discrimination, for DHS, it's 54.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: For HRA, it's 7 for a total of 61.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Within the shelter system for TGNC discrimination for DHS, 24, for HRA, zero, and that's 24.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: For TGNC harassment within shelter, for DHS, five, for HRA, zero, so a total of five.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And then for TGNC retaliation in the DHS shelter system, that's two.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: HRA, that's zero.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And for a total of two.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: So a total of 92 reports across that system.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And just to note, those figures reflect concern.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Concerns raised through the IQ system that may come through the ombudsman 311.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: NYC.gov, one number, mail, walk-in, email, does not include 911 calls, provider-level managed complaints, other incidents.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Do you track those other incidents, though?

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I'm going to defer to Deputy Commissioner Russell on whether we have access to the shelter level incidents.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And 911 calls is certainly something I think I mentioned in a previous hearing.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I'm trying to make sure we have ready access to across all of our shelters.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: Thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: So, yes, as it relates to the shelter-related incidents, while I don't have that specific data with me today, we do track those incidents that are reported at a shelter level.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: You do track them?

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: We do.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Do you have any numbers to share?

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: I actually do.

chapters.summary: Dalton provides FY25 data: 57 gender discrimination, 7 TGNC discrimination, 5 TGNC harassment, 6 TGNC retaliation. Farías asks about standard responses; Dalton describes investigations, corrective action plans, and shelter-specific interventions.
chapters.title: Farías questions DSS on FY25 numbers and response actions

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And also, I would love the FY25 numbers if you have them.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: As well.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: All right, so.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I can give you the FY25 numbers just as I did before.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: So the category.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Yeah, that would be great.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Yep.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: So starting with gender discrimination, DHS 46, HRA 11 for a count of 57.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: TGNC discrimination, DHS 7, HRA 0, total of 7.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: TGNC harassment, DHS 5, HRA 0 for a total of 5.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: TGNC retaliation, DHS 6, HRA 0 for a total of 6.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Yep.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And then I don't have, we can, as Deputy Commissioner suggested, we can get you priority one incidents that I know are tracked.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I do have them for ACE's place and Marsha's house, if you'd like those.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And what are the standard actions?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: You can follow up with us with those if you want.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Unless our council wants them to say it now on the record.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And what are the standard actions that DSS may take in response to these reports?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And I've noticed, obviously, that the majority of these complaints, almost the entirety of the complaints,

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: if I'm not mistaken, are in DHS as opposed to HRA.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And so specifically with regards to DHS,

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: What are you all doing to address these complaints and experiences?

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Yeah, I'll start, and if the Deputy Commissioner wants to jump in, I'd welcome that.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I mean, it's our responsibility to investigate all of these complaints, and if there are systemic issues to work across the whole system to ensure that we make improvements, and we do that through our DSS Office of LGBTQIA Plus Affairs.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: If there are shelter-specific issues, we certainly take those very seriously and work with the shelter to make changes and corrective action plans and so on.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And whenever possible, when we have the information, we do work to get that information back to the person who complained and work with them directly to share those results.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Okay, no follow-up.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thanks.

chapters.summary: Farías asks how many dedicated TGNC beds exist. Dalton reports 192 beds across four boroughs: 21 in Bronx, 5 in Brooklyn, 159 in Queens, and 7 in Manhattan, with no beds in Staten Island.
chapters.title: Farías questions DSS on dedicated TGNC shelter beds by borough

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: All right, how many dedicated shelter beds exist for transgender and gender nonconforming people across the five boroughs?

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I'll take that too.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Today there are 192 beds across four boroughs, so with two sites in the Bronx, one in Brooklyn, two in Queens, and two in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Can you give me the bed breakdown by borough?

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: So for the Bronx, 21 beds total.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Brooklyn, five beds total.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Queens, 159 beds total.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And Manhattan, seven beds total.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Farías asks about the Lopez v. DHS settlement requiring TGNC shelter units in every borough and anti-retaliation protections. Dalton describes staff training, nondiscrimination agreements, complaint tracking categories, and enhanced reporting at ACE's Place including QR codes and paper forms on every floor.
chapters.title: Farías questions DSS on Lopez settlement implementation

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Mariah Lopez versus New York City Department of Homeless Services resulted in settlement in which the city agreed to establish shelter units that serve and affirm

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: trans, non-binary, and gender non-conforming people in every borough and improve policies and procedures to protect TGNC clients.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Pursuant to this settlement, DHS is required to strengthen protections against retaliation for LGBTQIA plus residents who file complaints.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: How have you implemented this?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And are there plans to put any beds in Staten Island?

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: So the requirements in the Lopez settlement were specific to TGNC clients.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: All agency and provider staff are required to receive training specific to respectfully working with this population, and DHS requires that all new staff complete trainings within 90 days of hiring and refreshers every two years, as I mentioned.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: We also have all staff sign a nondiscrimination agreement and created designated categories to track TGNC retaliation complaints.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Note that for every floor at ACE's place, there is a way for people to complain.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I was out there recently and did take note compared to any other shelter that I've seen anywhere, how much care they take.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: There's a QR code on the elevators.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: There's a paper form on every single floor.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: So from my perspective, they go out of their way at ACE's place to ensure that people have opportunity to complain.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And also pursuant to the settlement, we took a number of steps to improve the reporting procedures.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: So we've adjusted the rights and responsibilities that every client receives and signs explicitly referring to the rights of LGBTQIA plus and TGNC clients.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: We've also, for every TGNC client receives a brochure about their rights and how to report concerns outside of their shelter.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: We updated our complaint tracking system to have designated categories.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: So those are some of the ways that we've complied.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I cannot speak to, but certainly part of any planning process, I can't speak to whether we are looking at additional beds in Staten Island, but certainly something I'll take note of.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'm going to pause briefly and turn it over to Councilmember Wilson to ask a question.

chapters.summary: Council Member Wilson asks about housing for LGBTQ+ seniors, citing 2017 data showing high poverty and substandard housing rates. Dalton and Russell describe 55+ and 65+ shelters with partnerships to senior service organizations, and Dalton commits to working with the aging commissioner.
chapters.title: Wilson questions DSS on LGBTQ+ older adults housing

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: I have some questions I think best for DSS as well.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: First is around LGBTQ older adults.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: In 2017, a survey of queer older adults, you know, nearly one quarter of the respondents earned less than 30% of area medium income.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: And nearly one in four of the respondents reported living in substandard housing and some even in shelters.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: So what steps can we take to ensure that seniors...

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: Have stable, safe housing where their medical and social needs are met?

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: And are there specific programs for LGBTQI plus seniors that may be in shelter?

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I'll start.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Yeah, I think across the country we've seen a rise in older adults facing homelessness.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And, you know, from my perspective, certainly not acceptable for our folks, older folks on fixed incomes to be.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: At this stage in their life trying to figure out where they're going to spend their next night.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And so I think all of our programs, diversion and so on, are, of course, available to older adults.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And I think it's a good point.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I can't speak to the ways we might be working with our colleagues in aging, but we have a great commissioner there that I'd love to work with to make sure people

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: understand what's available, understand how they might be able to stay successfully within their homes, what we might do to reduce entries into the system.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: So that's something I can take as a follow-up.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And it is nice to hear, again, being new to New York, that we have specific facilities and shelters for 55-plus, 65-plus.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And I suspect, but I'll let the deputy commissioner speak to it, that there are good partnerships with the senior centers and other aging systems within those shelters.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: But let me see if she has something to add.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: Thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: So as Commissioner Dawson started out with, our shelter providers do work.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: directly with those not-for-profits that perhaps offer assistance for our more mature population that again are 55 or 60 plus.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: So we give a level of autonomy to those providers to build those relationships with organizations that can tailor their services for those that are in that particular shelter.

chapters.summary: Wilson asks about updating Ryan White and HOPWA programs. John Rojas describes federal modernization changes to funding formulas, the HASA representative on the Ryan White Planning Council, and ongoing monitoring of federal budget developments.
chapters.title: Wilson questions on HIV/AIDS housing programs modernization

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: There are programs, now just pivoting to support with folks with HIV and AIDS, there's programs like the Ryan White Part A housing and HIV emergency shelter allowance that are really important ways to connecting people with HIV and AIDS to housing.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: But they're established a long time ago, usually in the height of the epidemic.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: And how can we update these programs for the needs of the present?

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Yeah, I'm going to defer there.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you.

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: So good morning, John Rojas.

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: You're right, a lot of the programs were created, Ryan White I believe was created in 1991, the Ryan White Part A grant, HAPWA equally in 1992.

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: Both Ryan White and HAPWA, the Housing Opportunity Persons Grades through the

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: U.S.

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: Department of Housing and Urban Development have modified and revised their programs recently.

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: HOPPO most recently did a HOPPO modernization.

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: In the past, they used to calculate how they gave money to eligible metropolitan statistical areas through AIDS only, not including

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: persons with HIV, and that was changed.

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: Also, in the past, they used to use statistical calculations, including persons who were deceased, instead of including persons who were living and newly diagnosed, but not AIDS diagnosed.

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: So luckily, that changed, and it really did change the portfolio of the services.

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: Actually, New York City took a hit, and probably not to popularly say this, but it should have more appropriately

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: divvied up the money to localities across the country who really did need the money more based on a growing number of cases in other states.

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: So there are...

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: On the federal level modernization and updates to the

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: So the programs on the local level, the HIV AIDS versus administration, HASA, has a participant who sits on the HIV Ryan White Planning Council.

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: That's the executive deputy commissioner, Jacqueline Dudley.

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: She sits on the Ryan White Part A Planning Council and gives input as overseeing HASA and the services that we provide on how we can update the services, including the aging population of folks living with HIV and AIDS.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: And then just one question about LGBTQ youth.

chapters.summary: Wilson asks about one-time cash payment programs to prevent youth homelessness. Thorne describes pilots including the Trust Youth Initiative with Ali Forney Center and Point Source Youth. Brown adds that direct assistance and wraparound services are needed, requiring sustained investment.
chapters.title: Wilson questions on cash payment programs for youth homelessness prevention

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: There have been some organizations that have advocated for targeting housing assistance programs that consist of one-time cash payments.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: To youth at risk of homelessness along with housing navigation and financial coaching and other forms of guidance in order to prevent youth homelessness.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: Just curious your opinions on the efficiency of that method and if that's something that's ever been considered.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: That's a great question.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: There's been a few pilots for young people who are experiencing homelessness, and

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: it's any resource that could help prevent homelessness and to support young people and to kind of...

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: Relieve the stress burden that they're experiencing by offering cash is a good idea.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: But at this moment, there's no program that DYCD is working on, is undergoing right now.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: The pilots that you mentioned, where have those taken place in the city here?

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: Yeah, they were partnerships with CBOs, and one was the Trust Youth Initiative with Alifone Center and Point Source Youth, and then Point Source Youth is also working on other

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: groups, and then there's one at Covenant House.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: And I've just told you just about all I know about how they're going.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: But, yeah, it's a good resource.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: In theory.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: And I'll say again, coming in, obviously, and still being new and still being planning, I think that ideas like that and pile initiatives are very smart.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: I think, again, in terms of the framing, as I put it down, when we look at sort of the drivers of homelessness and housing insecurity, and especially as that's broken down by race and socioeconomic status, direct assistance is what people need across the spectrum.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: And so I think, you know, we certainly have to be smart about that in terms of like when we say direct cash, but does that mean working directly with landlords or having contracts with specific

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: housing projects or creating those housing projects where we have sort of the direct ability to fund those things to make sure that people have the stability they need.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: But again, also tying into that pilot initiative and those kinds of programs, what I'm understanding is generally described as wraparound services.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Moving people towards independence and self-sustainability.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: So that means ensuring that people have the educational opportunities, have the employment opportunities to eventually become self-sufficient.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: But I will say just hearing like a one-time shot, and I think there's something, there's programs like that, I think it requires a more sustained investment.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Okay, back to DSS.

chapters.summary: Farías asks about increased oversight for LGBTQ+ shelter residents. Russell describes proactive measures including Code of Conduct mandates, required trainings, and reactive measures including data review, provider conversations, transfers, and on-site visits. Farías emphasizes staff should be moved before residents when complaints involve staff.
chapters.title: Farías questions DSS on oversight and accountability measures

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: So what increased oversight and accountability measures has DHS implemented to address discrimination and unsafe conditions for LGBTQ plus shelter residents?

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: So we take the dignity and safety of our clients seriously, and we continually monitor conditions to ensure shelter conditions are safe and supportive.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: And depending on the circumstances, our response are proactive and sometimes reactive.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: As it relates to the proactive side, our DHS Code of Conduct mandates that clients have the right not to be discriminated against due to their sexual orientation or gender identity and not be discriminated against by other clients.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: The trainings that DHS staff and providers are required to take speak to how to handle these complaints of discrimination from LGBTQI plus and TGNC communities.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: We monitor whether staff or providers have taken the required training.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: On the reactive side, we do review the data as it relates to incidences, and we have conversations with our providers when we notice that there's an uptick or a trend that's starting to occur, and we work on how to mitigate those concerns.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: Depending on the level of the complaint, we will make a transfer or other accommodations when we deem necessary as a result.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: Provider accountability is consistent in all cases.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: DHS shelter

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: staff will make regular on-site visits to these locations to ensure safety standards are being upheld by our providers.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And the transfer or accommodations, is that in reference to the residents or the staff?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Like if there's a complaint about a staff,

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: How are you addressing that?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Are you moving the resident or are you moving the staff?

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: So depending on the circumstances, it may be that we move the staff or it may be that we move the resident.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: Sometimes a resident may request to be transferred and we will accommodate them accordingly.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I just want to make the point that I think, and I'm sure you all share this, but sometimes what we say in theory doesn't always match what happens in practice.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And so I think it's important to start with the staff if a staff needs to be relocated, especially considering.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: You know, the shelter population and making sure that we're addressing whatever needs to happen at the staff level before we're moving residents that might be in shelter.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: And I agree with you.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Yeah, I knew you would.

chapters.summary: Farías asks how DSS addresses unique LGBTQ+ needs. Dalton highlights the DSS Office of LGBTQIA+ Affairs as an independent community-facing resource and the forthcoming TGNC advisory committee launching in fall 2026.
chapters.title: Farías questions DSS on addressing unique needs and advisory committee

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: How is DHS working to better address the unique needs of LGBTQIA plus individuals?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I guess you sort of.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Address that, but...

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I'll just add two things.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: It is...

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: a real asset to have the DSS office of LGBTQIA plus affairs to sit, of course, over both DHS and HRA and all of our programs and be another place where

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: we can be out in the community understanding the issues where that is separate from the shelter system and I hope is seen as independent from the rest of our services.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Understand those needs, again, be in community.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And also, again, just to mention, I mentioned this in my testimony, but we're really excited about the advisory committee that will be standing up later this fall for TGNC

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: identifying folks in particular to help inform us on how the system can

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: We can improve services across both DHS and HRA.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: So I think in addition to what we've already raised, those two assets I think will be important.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Public Advocate Williams delivers opening remarks highlighting that LGBTQ+ youth are 40% of unhoused youth, face high rates of violence and suicide, and that black trans women are disproportionately impacted. He calls for more affordable housing, lifts up organizations like Ali Forney and SAGE, and memorializes the late Lou Fiddler's advocacy on this issue.
chapters.title: Public Advocate Williams on LGBTQ+ housing crisis

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And I failed to acknowledge that we've also been joined by Council Member Nurse and Public Advocate Williams, who I believe would like to share some remarks.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: Thank you, ma'am.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: Hello, hello.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: Thank you, Madam Chair.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: Much appreciated.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: I'm still riding high, so I want to say Nixon 5.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: That's just how I say hello.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: Good afternoon, peace and blessings, love and light to everyone.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: My name is Jermaine Williams, public advocate for the city of New York.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: I'd like to thank Chair Farias, Chair Hudson, members of the Women and Gender Equity and General Welfare Committee for holding this hearing, for the opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: She shared testimony and the admin who's here as well.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: New York City is currently in the midst of an unparalleled housing crisis, one that is making it difficult for immigrants, young people in marginalized communities to obtain any sort of affordable housing.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: Rising costs and a lack of deeply affordable housing has slowly but surely pushed more and more New Yorkers from the communities and neighborhoods they have called home.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: I forgot to mention this is an opening statement in the middle of the hearings, but thank you for allowing me to make it.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: LGBTQIA plus individuals are disproportionately affected by the housing crisis and by discrimination in the rental and senior housing markets.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: Research done by Homeward NYC shows that despite representing 7% of the general population, LGBTQ plus youth make up roughly 40% of unhoused youth.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: They face high levels of harassment and violence on the street and shelter system.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: LGBTQ young people are more likely to engage in sex work or survival sex and are at high risk for sexual assault.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: LGBTQ plus youth consider suicide at about four times the rate of their heterosexual and cisgender peers, and they're up to four times more likely to attempt suicide.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: Studies have also shown that housing providers are less likely to respond to rental inquiries from same-sex couples,

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: as housing costs continue to rise, many LGBTQ plus seniors on fixed income struggle to remain in their homes and communities and thus are forced to look for alternative living arrangements.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: With many facilities run by provider organizations or religiously affiliated groups, LGBTQ plus elders frequently are at risk of risk.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: facing discrimination in assisted living facilities and or senior housing, and therefore face harassment or pressure to hide their sexual orientation or gender identity.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: With increasing attacks from the federal administration, transgender and gender nonconforming siblings face significant barriers in the traditional shelter system, often experience

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: targeted violence or a lack of appropriate resources, both to discrimination, but also due to lack of funding and resources.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: I do want to make sure I always highlight the intersection of LGBTQIA plus and being black and brown or immigrant.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: The intersection makes it even worse.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: I always want to highlight black trans women in particular who seem to have the brunt of what's happening.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: Housing is a human right, and as a city, we need to ensure that all New Yorkers have equal access to stable housing regardless of their sexuality or gender identity.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: Under New York City's human rights law, it is illegal for landlords, real estate agents, or co-op condo-owned boards to refuse housing, charge higher rent, or harass individuals based on sexual orientation, gender identity, or gender.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: And at least for now, fair housing laws also make discrimination based on HIV AIDS status or gender dysphoria illegal.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: I'll be remiss in not mentioning the efforts of organizations like Ali Forney, who established the B.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: Arthur House and an 18-bed transitional housing facility in Manhattan for LGBTQIA plus youth, and SAGE, who continues to support LGBTQIA plus seniors with housing.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: But we must endeavor to do more.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: We as a city must continue to do more.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: push more affordable and sustainable housing for LGBTQIA plus individuals with a particular focus on housing for the youth and seniors already at a higher risk of homelessness.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: Look forward to continuing the discussion today and how my office can continue to support this important work and how we as a city can continue to fight for more equitable New York for all.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: And I would say I think the results of the

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: races last night really highlight how important this is and how much New York wants to see everyone be able to be housed.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: Lastly, I do want to just lift up the name of the late Lou Filla.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: May he rest in peace.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: This type of housing was actually something that he was very big on, even though his community was not.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: So I just want to lift him up for the work that he did on this.

utterances.speaker: Jermaine Williams
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you, Public Advocate.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Okay, now back to our regularly scheduled hearing.

chapters.summary: Farías confirms staff training is every two years and asks about ACE's Place occupancy. Dalton reports 109 residents as of last week and clarifies ACE's Place opened in 2025, with 150 total beds.
chapters.title: Farías questions on staff training frequency and Ace's Place occupancy

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I just want to go back to the staff training.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: You said for new employees, they're required to do the training within 90 days of employment.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And then what is the subsequent training after that?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Is it annual?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Every two years.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Sorry, can you just say that into the microphone?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I can.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Every two years.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Ace's Place is the city's first trans and gender nonconforming dedicated shelter following the Lopez settlement and was opened in 2022.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Of the 150 beds available at Ace's Place, how many are currently occupied?

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Yes, as of last week, there were 109 individuals at Ace's Place, which I think opened in 2025.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: That's what I have.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Opened in 2025.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: That's what I have, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And sorry, I just wanted to reference something in your...

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Okay, I'll look for that in a second.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Yeah.

chapters.summary: Dalton explains ACE's Place referrals occur through intake gender identity screening and later self-identification. Farías briefly asks DYCD whether the 40,000 youth served annually are unique or duplicated; Thorne confirms counts include outreach contacts and are duplicated.
chapters.title: Farías questions on Ace's Place referrals and DYCD youth count

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: How are referrals made for ACE's place?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Yep.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I'll start, and certainly if the Deputy Commissioner wants to add anything.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: It's as we've described.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: At intake, we ask everyone if they would like to complete a gender identity screener, and if they...

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: If their identity suggests ACE's place, then we would make that offer amongst other considerations.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And just to also note, again, this was, I think, stated by the deputy commissioner, but likely we do have a number of folks who...

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Identify later, whether in their current shelter or to their case manager, in which case we, across all of our programs and shelters, would seek to make accommodations to have people in the right place.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And sorry, I just want to jump to DYCD for one second and just ask, of the 40,000 young people you serve annually,

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: are those all unique numbers or are they repeat clients?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Coming through like you might you count somebody more than once if they're seeking services more than once.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Yes, they would be duplicated Okay, do you know how many of the 40,000 are unique?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Or like a percentage?

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: A lot.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: We count outreach for outreach activities.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: And so the outreach, we don't have unduplicated outreach.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: There's thousands of people that we reach out to.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: The drop-in centers.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: Go out and do presentations at schools, other community-based organizations, and tabling events.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: So that's part of that $40,000.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thanks.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: If it's possible to break any of that down at a later date, we would love the follow-up.

chapters.summary: Farías raises reports of subpar conditions and harassment at ACE's Place. Dalton describes facility issues from burst pipes during code blue that were addressed, investigated harassment complaints resulting in additional staff training, and notes the shelter was physically nice during her visit. Brown flags ongoing litigation and plans to visit ACE's Place and other TGNC sites.
chapters.title: Farías questions on Ace's Place oversight and reported conditions

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And then just going back to ACE's place, what type of inspection and oversight is taking place there?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Is that where you mentioned you visited recently, Commissioner?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I just want to note that there have been recent reports of subpar living conditions, safety issues, and harassment.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: So it would be great if you can just provide an update on how DSS is handling these issues and what type of oversight you all are providing.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: It's my understanding that some of the complaints that you're referring to were related to facility issues when the pipes burst during the extended code blue.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And that was addressed expediently and clients were moved to rooms with heat and water.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: We certainly take all complaints very seriously.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: There's an assigned DHS program administrator who works closely with ACE's place to ensure compliance.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: There were also complaints about alleged client harassment that were investigated and resulted in continued discussions with clients regarding respecting gender identity and expression of other clients and staff.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And additional training for staff was put in place to better support the diversity of gender expression and gender identity there.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Yes, and as I said, in terms of the actual physical conditions of the location, I found the shelter to be very nice and went in several rooms.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: throughout the building, the offices, where the psychiatric nurse works, and so on.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And so from a physical perspective, I found ACE's place to be really nice with really nice outdoor space.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: But that, of course, doesn't speak to...

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: How people might be treated there, which again, we take very seriously.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: And I think just to highlight sort of the cooperation amongst agencies, coming into office, they took a survey of all litigations against the city dealing with LGBTQI issues, including there is ongoing litigation around that, around the settlement agreement specifically.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: And so I just want to flag that those issues were brought to our office as well, including the letter that was sent to the mayor back in February.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: So it's something that we have paid attention to.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: I've been in touch with the DSS director of LGBTQI affairs, Brent Whitfield, and have also planned a visit to ACE's place as well as the other dedicated sites for TGNC people.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And those are, you've scheduled them so they will be occurring or have you visited any of them as of yet?

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Well, one was actually supposed to happen today, but then I got to this hearing, so we're in the midst of rescheduling that.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: And the other ones will be scheduled.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: And also it's going to be an opportunity to meet directly with the people that are being housed and also the staff.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

chapters.summary: Hudson cites LL95 data showing only 478 LGBTQ+ individuals in DHS shelters (1% of 50,000), compared to 40% at DYCD. Dalton attributes the gap to voluntary, anonymous survey administration at intake. Russell explains LL95 counts those in TGNC beds, ACE's Place, Marsha's House, or self-identified TGNC clients.
chapters.title: Hudson questions on low LGBTQ+ count in DHS shelters, LL95 data

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: According to Local Law 95,

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Q4 of 2025 report, there were only 400

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: 78 LGBTQ plus individuals listed as being served by DHS when a typical day has at least 50,000 adults in shelter.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: This is only 1% of the shelter population, while by comparison, almost 40% of the clients DYCD serves in their shelter identify as LGBTQ+.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: What do you think the reason is for such a low LGBTQ plus population in adult shelters, especially given that many reports have found that LGBTQ plus individuals are more likely to be housing insecure than non-LGBTQ plus individuals?

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, I frankly think it's the way, of course, that we are required to administer the survey, which happens at intake, is anonymous and

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: not related, connected for lots of very good reasons to anyone's placement or services.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And so I think it's just a voluntary.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: While it's present and available, when people are doing and have all kinds of other issues going on in their lives, filling out a survey is probably not their primary concern.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And so I think, you know, I said it in my testimony, I think we acknowledge this well under counts.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: The people who would identify otherwise in a different manner.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And so I think we do, I think, treat the system, though, as if it is more diverse than what we see in that survey and seek to have the kind of services and supports that will support everyone.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And then how is data collected for that report?

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: What sources are used and how and at what point during intake does DSS ask for people to identify as LGBTQ plus?

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: So the data for LL95 is based on individuals who meet at least one of the following criterias.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: They are either in a TGNC designated bed, they are in ACE's place or Marsh's house, or they have self-identified as TGNC.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I'm going to kick it back to Chair Fedeas.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: To ask a few follow-up questions.

chapters.summary: Farías asks how DSS assesses training effectiveness. Russell acknowledges the agency tracks who completes trainings but does not currently have post-training assessment measures. Farías encourages implementing such evaluations.
chapters.title: Farías questions DSS on training efficiency assessment

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Yeah, I just have a couple questions based off of some responses.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: In terms of the staff trainings that occur, how are you assessing your training efficiency?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Is there like a post-case management referral?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Is it going off of any responses from any of the folks that...

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Are experiencing, you know, the case managers on site, what's the assessment look like?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: if any assessment is done at all?

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: That's a great question, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: Currently, we just track data as it relates to those that are taking the survey.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: So we have information that relates to the DHS staff that takes the survey, as well as the provider staff that takes the survey.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: But to your question, we don't have anything specific to track the post-training.

utterances.speaker: Sonia Russell
utterances.text: So that's a great question.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Yeah, I would encourage us to consider looking at that just because of the population we're serving.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: We want to make sure that there is a debrief within ourselves about what we're doing.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: So I would consider that.

chapters.summary: Farías urges agencies to be more direct in encouraging self-identification to enable early prevention, emphasizing that bureaucracy centers on data aggregation. Dalton agrees to partner on better data approaches. Brown raises the need to examine state and city privacy laws to protect LGBTQ+ data from federal hostile use.
chapters.title: Farías on data aggregation and direct communication for LGBTQ+ identification

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And then I, you know, I just, I don't know, I've kind of been wrecking my brain here about like the data aggregation, the data collection.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I understand to what you stated earlier around the sensitivity with not having to force anyone to identify just because of a potential resource or anything like that.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I do think.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: As a city, we should be more of the direct communicator, whether it's with the outreach or the encouragement to get people

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: to be encouraged to identify as soon as we can, because that's like the early, that is the way we can have early prevention or the earliest prevention marker.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: So whether we're being aggressive or having direct communication, I do think it should be from us because the worst outcome that could happen is that people still choose to just not, you know, check that box or, you know, self-identify.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And I'd rather us not be the, we want to consider.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: The sensitivity of it and rather just be really, really direct in that communication so we can get people the services that they deserve.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And really, I think the point I wrote for myself was at the end of the day, bureaucracy really is centered around

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: data aggregation and whether it's through 301 data that we're aggregating and in a local community or whether it's for you know serving our LGBTQ plus neighbors

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: with direct services and resources I really just want us to think about

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: how we're going to make a generational shift for the next generation with the time that we have in front of us now through the services we're providing or through the budgetary fights that we're trying to

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: gain every budget cycle or through readapting our data aggregation because it is the point that we use to

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: to fund and operate everything in the city.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: So I just really would appreciate you folks thinking more about

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: that and creating generational shifts.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Yeah, just to comment, and there might be other comments as well.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I mean, I agree, and I think just to separate the two things, the survey itself is not related to people's care and accommodations.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: I'm sure you know that, but I'm certainly open to partnering

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: with folks in the community across this city to think about how we might

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
