utterances.text: otherwise, in a safe and effective manner, understand the population that we're serving better because this is not yielding that kind of information such that we have to have other information to augment it.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Yeah, and I'll just add, I think, a part of this, and I sort of mentioned this in my testimony and sort of assessing current agency data practices and gaps in research that's needed, because ultimately, I think, in terms of the ideas that I have in advancing

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: rights and resources, I think, across the spectrum, it really is heavy in data.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: I think about, especially when you think about it from a litigation risk and litigation perspective and how we move legislation.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: But one component I think that certainly needs to be assessed and is something that I'm trying to make time to do is looking at our privacy laws.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: I think with the NYU-Lingon situation, we've now come to understand sort of the reach the federal government can have, even if it is.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Hopefully will be held unlawful as it has in 20 other instances, 21 now I believe.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: But I think we need to examine the extent that both our state and

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: our city privacy laws, the ways in which we can use them to protect the kind of information that we need whenever it's used for a hostile purpose specifically.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: I think that's well within the powers of the state and within the city, you know, through, well, at least as I understand the law.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: And so I think that's something that both city council and I think the mayoral administration would certainly be looking into and something I've prioritized amongst my ever-growing priority list.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Yeah, I appreciate your responses.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'll now pass it over to Councilman Berners for questions.

chapters.summary: Council Member Brewer asks about pathways for undocumented LGBTQ+ youth aging out of services. Thorne describes expanded 21-24 beds and the state Housing Access Voucher Program open to undocumented individuals. Dalton adds the SHARE rental assistance program, and Brown describes inherited work on resources for migrant LGBTQIA New Yorkers under Local Law 73.
chapters.title: Brewer questions on undocumented youth transitioning out of shelter

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I just have one question, which might lead to another one.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: This is based on an experience I had with a young person who was in a LGBTQ home in the city, undocumented youth.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So what happens after, I believe after he turned 21, he kind of was not able to continue to participate with a lot of services.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: What happens with undocumented youth who are transitioning out of shelter, who can't get into NYCHA or things like that?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: What kind of programs or pathways are set up so that they can be successful in the city, at least in securing housing?

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: Yeah, that's a great question.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: And because of the recent surge in immigrants who came in recently, it's been on our minds a lot.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: The resources, so we've been expanding the number of shelter beds that are available for, residential beds that are available for 21 to 24-year-olds, so that helps.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: Prolong the amount of time that people can stay in our services.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: At DYCD, we also, the state has recently

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: released housing program called the Housing Access Voucher Program that immigrants are eligible for, undocumented individuals are eligible for.

utterances.speaker: Tracy Thorne
utterances.text: So that's something that is available to New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: No, thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: This was probably like 2017, 2018.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: This was before our terrible president right now, or actually after, but it wasn't like this.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I think it's just interesting to see that there's, I'm glad to hear that, but a gap in how are folks going to be able to get set up and work and get a housing if they're by themselves and have been by themselves for a long time.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: Councilmember, I'd like to offer additional supports that are offered through DSS.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: As you know, we're committed to connecting New Yorkers to stable housing and keeping them stably housed.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: We do have a rental assistance program called SHARE, Special Housing Assistance Resources, funded by the state.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And it is specific for individuals or households that are in shelter that can't access the other government subsidies.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: And so that's available to that population.

utterances.speaker: Erin Dalton
utterances.text: It is provided irrespective of orientation or expression.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: And I just want to add, too, as part of, and I think I mentioned this before you came in, as part of the inherited work the Mayor's Office of LGBTQIA Affairs has inherited from the Unity Project in collaboration with the Mayor's Office of Immigration Affairs under Local Law 73.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: We're currently working on our rights and resources specifically for migrant LGBTQIA New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: And so this will be something.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: That we specifically address and including the ways that we disseminate that information to make sure people are aware of the resources and that we're being proactive about it.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: I know that the report to council is due in November, I believe, but I think, you know, we're trying to get ahead of schedule as we can.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

chapters.summary: Hudson asks what percent of HASA clients identify as LGBTQ+. John Rojas reports DSS does not collect sexual orientation data but does collect transgender/non-binary data, with about 5% (approximately 1,800 clients) identifying as TGNC.
chapters.title: Hudson questions on HASA client LGBTQ+ identification

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I'd like to shift to HASA.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Does HRA know what percent of HASA clients also identify as LGBTQIA plus?

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: We don't collect data on sexual orientation, though.

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: We do collect data on transgender, non-binary, and gender non-conformance.

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: That is 5%, about 1,800 or so clients identify as transgender, gender non-conforming, or non-binary.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Sorry, 5% of 1,800 or 5% is equivalent to 1,800?

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: Is equivalent to 1,800 or so.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: Of the total HASA caseload.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Hudson asks about contingency plans if HOPWA or Ryan White funding is eliminated. Rojas confirms ongoing monitoring of federal budget developments, conversations with OMB, and advocacy to maintain services should funding be discontinued.
chapters.title: Hudson questions on federal funding risk for HIV services

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Federal funding for HIV services is at risk, including HAPA and the Ryan White HIV program.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: HASA's housing program uses federal funding from the HAPA program, and there have been recent proposals to eliminate those grants entirely, incorporating this funding into larger block grants.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Does HRA have a plan for how it will preserve services for this population should federal funding be limited or discontinued?

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: Luckily, in the prior year, both programs were at risk and continue to be funded till today, so that's good news.

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: But we're watching the developments in the federal budget closely.

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: Should HOP be discontinued, we will work closely with our partners in the city and federal level to ensure our funding needs are being met and to maintain these services.

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: Really hoping and advocating for that.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Are you flagging this for OMB?

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: So they are aware that should federal funding be eliminated, that they would have to fill in the gap?

utterances.speaker: John Rojas
utterances.text: Those conversations are ongoing with OMB.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Hudson asks about Local Law 130 reporting. Rima Rivera reports CY2024 data: 16,318 total survey respondents with 16% identifying as LGBTQI+, including 25% of 114 DVS respondents, 16% of 8,906 cash assistance respondents, 18% of 915 IDNYC respondents, and 15% of 6,383 SNAP respondents.
chapters.title: Hudson questions on LL130 DV shelter survey data for CY2024

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Local Law 130 of 2021 requires that DSS, quote, complete a review of the services and resources provided to LGBTQI plus

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: individuals entering domestic violence emergency shelters, including but not limited to efforts aimed at data collection about LGBTQI plus individuals utilizing domestic violence emergency shelter

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: and shall submit to the mayor and the speaker of the city council and post on its website a written report, including the following information.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Quote, how many people were offered the survey and what percent of those offered the survey responded?

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: I'm Vima Rivera, Deputy Commissioner for HRA's Domestic Violence Services.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: So we don't track how many people were offered the survey, but I can tell you that for our submission for calendar year 2024, which was submitted in 2025,

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: There were 16,318 responses to the survey.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: Of those who responded, 16% identified as LGBTQI+.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: There were 114 DVS respondents, 25% of whom identified as LGBTQI.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I'm sorry, what was that last number?

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: DBS respondents, of which 25% identified as LGBTQI.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: There were 8,906 cash assistance respondents, of which 16% identified as LGBTQ.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I'm sorry, I'm moving slowly here.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Can you repeat that last number?

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: There were 8,906 cash assistance respondents.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And that percentage was?

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And sorry, the first statistic, 16,318 with 16%, what was that for specifically?

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: How many respondents?

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: Total.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: Right, in total.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: Respondents to the survey.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Cash assistance was the last one?

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: There's a couple more.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: There was 915 ID NYC respondents.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: That's 915, of which 18% identified as LGBTQI.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: And 6,383 SNAP respondents.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: Of which 15% identified as LGBTQI.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: That was for calendar year 2024.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: That was submitted in 2025.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Do you have calendar year 2025?

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: We're in the process of gathering that information.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: It would be great if you could follow up with us whenever you have that.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: When do you usually have that available?

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: Actually, they just reached out to my office a couple of weeks ago.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: It's due in August, but they tapped my shoulder.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: As I start gathering.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

chapters.summary: Hudson asks how personal information is protected and whether respondents express concerns. Rivera confirms data is voluntary, confidential, and anonymous, and says she is not aware of any elevated concerns about the survey.
chapters.title: Hudson questions on data privacy and respondent concerns

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And can you just share how personal information is protected among these respondents?

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: So this information is voluntary, it's kept confidential, it's anonymous, so this information is not shared.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Do any respondents express concern about completing the survey in relation to retaliation or data privacy?

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And if so, how do you manage those concerns?

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: I'm not aware of anyone who has elevated any concerns regarding the survey,

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: but I would imagine that if there were concerns, we would again emphasize that these responses are confidential, they're anonymous, and that they serve to guide and inform our work.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Hudson notes HRA reported zero LGBTQ+ complaints in CY2022 and asks about general complaint numbers. Rivera explains DV shelters are regulated by OCFS, which receives complaints directly, and HRA is not privy to them without client consent.
chapters.title: Hudson questions on DV shelter complaints and OCFS oversight

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And for the calendar year 2022 report, HRA reported that in calendar year 2022, DVS did not receive any inquiries or complaints regarding the survey.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Domestic violence emergency shelter services provided to residents who identified as LGBTQIA+.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: This includes any inquiries or complaints forwarded via the intranet quorum or IQ system.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Do you know how many complaints regarding DV emergency shelters did DHS receive during this period in general?

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: At this time, our program, I can't give an accurate count of the emergency shelter complaints in 2022 through our IQ system, but I do also want to note.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: That our shelters are overseen by OCFS.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: They receive operating certificates, and OCFS regulates our shelters.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: And if there are any complaints, the residents usually submit those complaints directly to OCFS, and HRA, serving as the local social service district, does not have

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: any, not privy to what these complaints are, unless the client signs consent for us to know of them.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Hudson asks whether low complaints reflect no concerns or reluctance to self-identify. Rivera acknowledges the importance of building trust before asking for disclosure, suggesting information be collected after initial crisis subsides, and emphasizes improving how questions are asked.
chapters.title: Hudson questions on low complaint rates and encouraging disclosure

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Can you just...

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Assess or opine on whether or not you think the lack of complaints is because there were no concerns from LGBTQI plus identified residents or because complaint complainants choose not to self-identify?

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: I can't say one way or another, but I can say that I think that that's it's so important for us to kind of message the importance of

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: of disclosure.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: It's, and also emphasize that it's voluntary and we understand it's deeply personal, but the goal is really, although we don't want to pressure self-disclosure, I think there are ways in which we can encourage self-disclosure.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: I think the way we ask

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: clients to identify is very important.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: I think we need to also, for instance, with our DV providers, that when a client comes to a DV shelter, they're in the moment of crisis.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: They may not be of the mindset to share that information.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: So perhaps it would be best to obtain this information once we both trust and to come back and enter that information in our database so that we can have that information.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: So I understand.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: why the numbers are so low at time of intake, but I think we just need to encourage that we build trust and let clients know why this information is needed, ask it in a way where it's welcomed

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: to receive, and make sure that it's documented.

utterances.speaker: Rima Rivera
utterances.text: In our systems, data systems.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Hudson thanks the administration witnesses, encourages continued proactive assessment and addressing of LGBTQ+ needs in the shelter system, and notes the strong interagency collaboration demonstrated during the hearing.
chapters.title: Hudson closing remarks to administration panel

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I think that concludes our question section, but I do want to just note before I turn it back over to Chair Fideas, you know, I know that you all are all

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: willing partners in this work.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And I just want to encourage you to continue to be proactive in how we're assessing and addressing and serving the needs of

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: all of our unique populations in the shelter system, but particularly the LGBTQIA plus TGNC populations.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: It seems like there's great collaboration across the board and through all of the agencies.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And so I hope that that work continues.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And thank you all for your testimonies today.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Farías thanks the administration panel, transitions to public testimony, and reminds attendees of decorum rules, two-minute testimony limits, and procedures for submitting written testimony within 72 hours.
chapters.title: Farías thanks administration and announces public testimony rules

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Yeah, we're super appreciative of all of you showing up and answering all of our questions and obviously want to remain active partners with you.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: We're all trying to consistently remain proactive versus reactive, especially in government.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: So thank you all again.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And this portion of the hearing, you all are free to go.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Well, it would be nice if you stick.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: around and listen to some of the public testimony.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: We are now going to open the hearing for public testimony.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: As folks are leaving the dais, I would like to remind members of the public that this is a government proceeding and that decorum shall be observed at all times.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Members of the public shall remain silent unless called upon to testify.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: All testimony must be relevant to today's hearing on housing instability among LGBTQ plus individuals in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'm going to repeat that.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: All testimony must be relevant to today's hearing on housing instability among LGBTQ individuals in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: If remarks stray from the subject matter, speakers will be reminded of the topic and asked to refocus their comments.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: The witness table is reserved for people who wish to testify.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: No video recording or photography is allowed from the witness table.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Further, members of the public may not present audio or video recordings as testimony, but may submit transcripts of such recordings to the sergeant at arms for inclusion in the hearing record.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: If you wish to speak at today's hearing and you have not done so, please fill out an appearance slip with the sergeant at arms and wait to be recognized.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: When recognized, you will have two minutes to speak on today's oversight topic and or legislation.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Friendly reminder, you can submit a longer testimony, but you are reserved to just two minutes.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: We read all testimony that is submitted.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: If you have a written...

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: statement or additional written testimony you wish to submit for the record, please provide a copy of that testimony to the Sergeant at Arms.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: You may also email written testimony to testimony at council.nyc.gov within 72 hours of this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Audio and video recordings will not be accepted.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I will now call the first panel.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'd like to call Alex Guevara, Reddy Chhatniya Gujarati, and Nettalyn Helena Diaz to the dais.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Apologies for any mispronunciations.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: One more time.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Alex Guevara, Reddy, Chatnia, Gudrakti, and Nettaline Helena Diaz.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Alex, you can begin.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: You have to click the button on the mic and it'll light up for you.

chapters.summary: Alex Guevara, a queer and trans youth advocate, shares their experience of family rejection leading to RHY services, sleeping in drop-in center chairs, and being dead-named at Marsha's House. They credit city-funded new beds at AFC for reducing their wait time and call for vouchers and LGBTQ-affirming staff.
chapters.title: Alex Guevara (Ali Forney Center): Youth homelessness experience

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: Oh, got you.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: Thank you for letting me know.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: Hi everyone, my name is Alex Guevara and I'm a youth advocate at the Ali Fornay Center.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: I'm speaking as a queer and trans person who is living through what many LGBTQ plus young people have experienced.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: For LGBTQ youth, homelessness is so much deeper than not having a place to sleep.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: It's about rejection,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: instability, and survival.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: This is also the case for me as family rejection caused me to need HRHY services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: These experiences caused a lot of pain and I could not work on healing while waiting for a steady place to stay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: I arrived at AFC last summer at 21 years old.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: I stayed overnight at the drop-in center and on the streets for three weeks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: The drop-in center was not comfortable since they are no longer allowed to have beds, so I had to sleep in chairs or on top of a table.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: My case manager then referred me to Marsha's house.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: A DHS shelter, and I stayed there for two weeks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: Marsha's felt more like a prison.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: We were yelled at by staff while going about our daily activities, and we were dead named.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: It was made clear that we were just another case to solve, but we're not just that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: We're people with trauma, but most importantly, with stories that deserve to be respected and heard.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: If they hired LGBTQ staff, it would have been a much better environment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: Thanks to the city funding of 100 new beds for 21 to 24-year-olds, AFC was able to open up a new emergency housing site.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: Without that site opening, I easily could have been on the wait list for up to six months instead of a month.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: Today, I live in AFC's transitional housing program, and I'm looking into rapid rehousing or supportive housing as my next step.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: But the options are extremely limited, and the supportive housing process will take at least another year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: If youth had access to vouchers, I could have easily moved out on my own sooner.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: To end, I just want to say we as queer youth deserve a housing program that feels like home, where we can find community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: and where we can feel comfortable, we'll welcome you staff and peers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Alex Guevara
utterances.text: Thank you for your time.

utterances.speaker: Taylor Brown
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony.

chapters.summary: Chetanya Gunrathi of the NYC Youth Action Board testifies about disproportionate LGBTQ+ youth homelessness (40% of unhoused youth), challenges for LGBTQ+ immigrant youth, and urges expanding housing options and establishing a $15 million LGBTQIA+ immigration fund.
chapters.title: Chetanya Gunrathi (Youth Action Board): LGBTQ+ youth homelessness

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: Good morning, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: Thank you for giving me this opportunity to testify today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: My name is Chetanya Gunrathi, and I'm a member of the Youth Action Board of New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: The Youth Action Board is made up of young people with lived experience of homelessness.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: Our goal is to help shape programs, policies that affect runaway and homeless youth in New York City and to ensure that their voices are heard.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: I'm here today because I know firsthand how difficult it is to navigate housing instability as a young person.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: When you do not know where you're going to sleep at night, everything becomes harder, like finding a job, continuing your education, taking care of your health, and so on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: Throughout my work with the YAB, I have learned that LGBTQIA plus youth face some of the highest risks of homelessness.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: While they represent estimated 5 to 10% of overall youth population, they make up as much as 40% of youth experiencing homelessness.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: Many are pushed out by family rejection, discrimination, unsafe home environments, etc.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: Despite this, this prep

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: Proportionate need, resources, and funding have not kept pace, leaving many young people without access to stable and affirming support.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: For LGBTQIA plus immigrant youth, these challenges are often even more severe.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: Many come from countries where they face discrimination, persecution, or criminalization because of their identity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: After arriving in the United States, they often encounter additional barriers to housing, legal services, health care, and community support.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: However, there is no dedicated city funding stream specifically focused on the LGBTQIA plus immigrant communities, leaving some of our most vulnerable youth without targeted affirming services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: This is why I support expanding housing options for LGBTQIA plus youth and increasing funding for organizations that provide affirming services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: I urge the Council to expand LGBTQIA plus youth housing, increase investment in affirming services, and establish the proposed $15 million LGBTQIA.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: Care Place Immigration Fund.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: No young person should have to face homelessness alone because of their identity or immigration status.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: Thank you very much for your time.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Click your microphone.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Yep.

chapters.summary: Nedeleyn Helena Diaz, co-chair of the NYC Youth Action Board and outreach specialist with Housing Works, cites Trevor Project data showing 28% of LGBTQ+ youth experience housing instability, with 40% kicked out due to identity. She supports the $15M LGBTQ+ immigration fund and $10M for persons in the sex trade.
chapters.title: Nedeleyn Helena Diaz: Housing instability and LGBTQ+ youth

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: Good morning, but it's not morning.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: Good afternoon, chair and members of the council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: My name is Nedeleyn Helena Diaz, and I am the co-chair of the New York City YAB Youth Action Board and an outreach specialist with Housing Works LGBTQ+.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: Housing assistance program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: Through my work with Housing Works, I connect LGBTQ individuals experiencing homelessness or housing instability with housing resource, supportive services, and pathways to stability.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: In my outreach, I've met many young people who have been rejected by their families, forced to leave their homes, or left without a safe place to go simply because of who they are.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: I have worked with youth sleeping on friends'couches, cycling through shelters, or trying to survive without any support system.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: The emotional toll of rejection, discrimination, and housing instability is something no young person should have to endure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: Unfortunately, these stories are far too common.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: According to the research from the Trevor Project, 28% of LGBTQ plus youth have experienced homelessness or housing instability.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: Among youth,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: who were kicked out of their homes, 40% said it was because of their LGBTQ identity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: More than half of those who ran away reported doing so because of mistreatment or fear of mistreatment related to their identity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: These experiences have lasting consequences.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: LGBTQ,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: Youth experiencing homelessness or housing instability were found to have two to four times greater odds of reporting depression, anxiety, self-harm, and suicide attempts compared to those with stable housing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: That is why I support the coalition's call for a $15 million LGBTQIA plus immigration fund.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: Many LGBTQIA plus immigrants face compounded barriers, including housing instability, legal insecurity, language barriers, and difficulty assessing

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: affirming services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: I also support the 10 million supports for persons involved in the sex trade.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: Housing is more than shelter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: It is safety, opportunity, and hope.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: I urge the council to continue investing in solutions that ensure every New Yorker has access to safe and stable housing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nedeleyn Helena Diaz
utterances.text: Thank you for your time and the opportunity to testify.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you folks for coming in to testify today.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: You are now excused from the dais.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: We have no questions.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I would like to call up the following folks to testify.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'm trying my best to read these cards, folks.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Asher Blachany, I can't see the last couple letters, apologies.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Brian J.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Ellicott-Cook, Nadia Swanson, Eva Jacro, and Ethan Lowe.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Is there an Asher present?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Is there an Asher on the dais?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Okay, so Brian, you can begin.

chapters.summary: Brian Ellicott-Cook of SAGE testifies that 40% of LGBTQ+ older adults live below poverty, older adult homelessness has surged 250% since 2004, and affirming housing supply remains extremely limited. He warns HUD proposals threaten progress and recommends safety signals for self-identification.
chapters.title: Brian Ellicott-Cook (SAGE): LGBTQ+ older adults housing

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: Good afternoon, chairs, members of the committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: Thank you for this opportunity to speak on housing instability among LGBTQ plus New Yorkers, particularly LGBTQ plus older adults.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: My name is Brian Ellicott-Cook, and I serve as the Director of Government Relations at SAGE.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: SAGE is the country's oldest and largest organization dedicated to improving the lives of LGBTQ plus older adults here in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: We provide housing, supportive services, and community-based program to thousands of older adults each year, including through our LGBTQ plus affirming housing developments like Stonewall House in Brooklyn and Cortona Pride House in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: And yet LGBT New Yorkers are far more likely to experience housing instability.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: Nationally, approximately 40% of LGBT plus older adults live below the poverty line.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: And fewer than half, just 49%, own their own homes, compared to 65% of the general population.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: These disparities are driven by a lifetime of discrimination in employment, housing, and access to benefits, combined with higher rates of social isolation and the greater likelihood of aging without traditional family support.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: Here in New York City, broader homelessness crises among older adults underscore the urgency of these challenges.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: The number of individuals age 55 and older in the shelter system has increased approximately 250% between 2004 and 2017.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: With those aged 65 increasing more than 300%.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: Without intervention, that population is projected to continue to grow significantly in the coming years within the already vulnerable group of LGBTQ plus older adults who face greater risk.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: of housing insecurity, discrimination, and unsafe placement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: At this time, there are very limited affirming housing options available, while New York City has allowed us to make those developments at Stonewall House in Brooklyn and Crotone in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: The total supply of affirming housing for LGBT holder adults remains extremely limited and very much needed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: However, HUD's recent proposal to eliminate the extra access tool threatens to reverse all of this progress, and we must be prepared to fight for that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: As this committee examines housing insecurity among LGBT plus individuals, there is one thing I want to recommend.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: The agency's idea of safety signals for more people to self-identify in those first moments to an ally or a community member would make all the difference.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

chapters.summary: Nadia Swanson reports 400 youth on AFC's wait list (up from 300 last year, under 100 when vouchers existed), 25 deaths since 2020, and a funding structure where DYCD pays $50K per bed against $80K real cost. She demands raising bed rates, restoring vouchers, and baselining $5M in speaker initiative funding.
chapters.title: Nadia Swanson (Ali Forney Center): Youth homelessness crisis and funding gaps

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: My name is Nadia Swanson, pronouns they, them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: I'm the Director of Advocacy and Global Programs at the Aliforne Center.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: Tonight, hundreds of LGBT 16 to 24-year-olds will sleep on the streets or in drop-in center chairs, waiting six months for a crisis bed after escaping unfathomable levels of abuse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: Without stability, they're at further risk for criminalization, sexual violence, decompensating mental and physical health, substance misuse, and profound developmental harm.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: 90% of AFC's youth are BIPOC and come from low-income households.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: 20% of the youth at Rikers are LGBT.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: And since 2020, we have grieved 25 deaths.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: And those are just the ones we know of.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: LGBT unhoused youth die at more than double the rates of their peers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: These connections are not coincidental.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: We serve over 2,000 youth a year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: We have 400 on a wait list for a bed, and last year that number was 300.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: When youth had access to vouchers, it was under 100, and youth moved into stable housing with an increase by 350%.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: Now there are none, and there's a bottleneck.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: Statewide, RHY rose more than 70% in a single year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: The funding structure guarantees failure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: DYCD pays 50K per bed per year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: The real cost is 80K.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: That gap has real human consequences.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: The state and city exec cut RHY funding.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: The mayor's housing plan did not include any housing youth can access through DYCD.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: And HUD proposed gutting the equal access rule.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: The shameful thing is this is fixable.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: And it's not expensive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: We've been telling you what works.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: Youth homelessness can be rare, brief, non-recurring, and safe.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: We need the city to raise the RHY beds to 80K, fund LGBT youth mental health beds, increase crisis beds to eliminate wait lists.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: We need more funding for case management and therapy, restore DYCD voucher access and expand supportive housing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: Baseline, the $5 million speaker initiative, fund $15 million for LGBT immigrant legal services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: Youth homelessness is a choice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: New York City cannot call itself safe for LGBT youth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: Well, 400 of them are decompensated on a wait list.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: I'm tired of begging for the government to show it cares with action.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: This should be top priority.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: You can be the administration that eliminates wait lists.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: And I have a lot more I can say, as you know.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: So if you have questions, let me know.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you, and please submit anything additional that is helpful to your testimony.

chapters.summary: Ava Jacru, a case manager at a DHS Safe Haven, testifies that IDNYC requires photo ID to obtain photo ID, creating an impossible barrier for homeless clients. She proposes adding DHS to the caretaker voucher list so case managers can verify identity, unblocking housing applications for thousands.
chapters.title: Ava Jacru: IDNYC barriers trapping shelter clients in homelessness

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: Hello, my name is Ava Jacru.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: I'm a case manager at one of New York City's safe havens, facilities that serve as a last resort safety net for our most vulnerable neighbors, including LGBTQ plus youth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: Every day I work alongside New Yorkers who are ready to leave shelter behind and rebuild their lives in permanent housing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: But I am here today because my colleagues and I across the city are hitting a wall, an administrative catch-22 that leaves thousands of people trapped in homelessness for no reason other than a missing piece of plastic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: Here is the problem in its simplest form.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: IDNYC requires a photo ID to get a photo ID.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: Among my clients that are affected by this barrier,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: 100% of my queer clients are among this group.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: For someone with a stable home and a file drawer full of documents, this makes sense.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: But for populations we serve in safe havens, runaway and homeless youth, survivor of domestic violence, immigrants fleeing danger, people whose belongings have been swept away or destroyed, and individuals living with severe mental illness, this requirement is an impossible barrier.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: These are New Yorkers who have never had an ID or who have had their documents stolen, withheld as a tool of abuse, or discarded during a street sweep.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: Without a government-issued photo ID, they cannot apply for the very programs designed to lift them out of homelessness, the 2010E supportive housing, HRA benefits, public assistance, city FEPS, or SOTA.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: Currently, IDNYC

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: does allow certain caretakers to vouch for a client's identity in lieu of a photo ID.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: But the list of eligible agencies is narrow, and in what I believe to be a profound oversight, it does not include the Department of Homeless Services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: We are the very agency charged with housing this population, yet we are locked out of the process that could unblock their path to housing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: IDNYC currently accepts vouchers only from the NYS Office for People with Developmental Disabilities, the NYS Department of Health, the NYS Office of Mental Health, and the NYC Department of Health and Mental Hygiene.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: DHS Safe Havens, which serve as a catch-all for New York's most marginalized residents, are absent from this list.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: What I propose is that we add DHS to the caretaker list, allow DHS case managers who have established ongoing relationships with their clients and access to verified case records to waive the photo requirement for IDNYC.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: This simple change would immediately unblock housing for thousands of shelter clients.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: We are already carrying the trust and documentation to vouch for our clients'identities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ava Jacru
utterances.text: We simply need the city to recognize that authority.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: You can begin when ready.

chapters.summary: Ethan Lowe of the Legal Aid Society recommends expanding affirming shelter capacity across all boroughs, increasing awareness of existing TGNC placement rights, and expanding DSS LGBTQ Affairs capacity, noting clients still face problems even in designated affirming shelters due to insufficient staff training.
chapters.title: Ethan Lowe (Legal Aid Society): Affirming shelter access and staff training

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: Good afternoon, members of the committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: My name is Ethan Lowe, pronouns he, him, and I'm a staff attorney with the Legal Aid Society's LGBTQ Law and Policy Unit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: Based on Legal Aid's work in representing New Yorkers experiencing housing instability, we offer several recommendations to improve access to safe and affirming shelter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: Shelter is often the first step towards stable housing, but many LGBTQ individuals, especially transgender, gender nonconforming, non-binary New Yorkers, avoid or leave shelters because they do not feel safe.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: We commend the city for opening ACE's Place, the first transitional housing program for TGNC and B.I.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: However, Legal Aid continues to hear from TGNC and B.I.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: Clients who struggle to access affirming placements.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: The city should continue expanding affirming shelter capacity across all boroughs so that every TGNC and BI New Yorkers can access safer shelter when they need it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: We also urge the city to increase awareness of existing rights and resources.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: Many TGNC and BI individuals do not know that they can request placements in affirming shelters and TGNC units or request accommodations related to their gender identity and medical needs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: We routinely hear from clients who remain in unsafe placements simply because they are unaware that safer options existed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: The city shelters should have clear multilingual notices of

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: rights and have more proactive outreach at intake and throughout a client's shelter stay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: Some clients hesitate to disclose their identities without understanding its purpose and knowing about the placements available.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: Finally, the city should expand the capacity of DSS LGBTQ affairs and ongoing comprehensive LGBTQ training for shelter staff.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: Legal Aid continues to receive reports from LGBTQ clients, especially TGNC and BI clients, who experience problems even in DHS shelters designated as affirming because some staff lack sufficient understanding of TGNC and BI identities and needs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: Additional and improved training would help ensure LGBTQ New Yorkers can safely remain connected to shelter and housing services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: DSS LGBTQ Affairs has been a valuable resource for LGBTQ clients and DHS shelters, but its limited capacity delays responses to urgent needs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: We recommend dedicating resources to expanding DSS LGBTQ Affairs and creating additional LGBTQ liaison positions throughout the shelter system.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ethan Lowe
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify.

chapters.summary: A council member asks Swanson to elaborate on capacity issues. She details 2,000 youth served annually, 212 beds, 400 on the wait list today versus under 100 when vouchers existed, and explains the bottleneck: youth can't move out because there's nowhere to go, while bed rates are too low for providers to expand.
chapters.title: Q&A with Nadia Swanson on capacity and wait list bottleneck

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Not it.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: You mentioned...

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Sorry, capacity?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: In your testimony, can you just speak a little bit more about the issues around that?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: Yeah, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: So we serve over 2,000 youth a year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: That's between our 24-hour drop-in center and our housing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: We have 212 beds that are a mix of emergency and transitional.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: We currently, as of today, have 400 youth on a wait list for that crisis bed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: When youth had access to vouchers, there was a period of about four years where youth had access, whether it be the FEPS pilots or through EHV, where we got below 100.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: We're in this bottleneck.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: Youth can't get in because youth aren't moving out.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And sorry, you said, can you just repeat the numbers again?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: 2,000 youth a year, 400 on a wait list today, 300 on our wait list last year, and 100 when we had access to vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: On that same note, I was writing a lot of notes during their questioning.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: During Councilmember Nurse's question, I think it was an important note that, and I'm glad she said the years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: So over 10 years ago, we were fighting with Lou Fiddler to raise the age that DYCD would fund beds for 21 to 24-year-olds.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: It didn't pass until 2018, so that also leads to what that question was with Councilmember Nurse.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: We had 40 open beds, 60 kind of, up until last year when we got that extra 100.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: AFC took on 45 of those new beds.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: 20 were of the ones that Alex spoke about.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: Other RHY programs have been hesitant and didn't take on the beds that we know we need because the bed rate is so low we can't afford to take them on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: And so this is like the biggest sort of crux of it all.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: And there's been this push to fund housing navigators and all of this, which is wonderful.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: We need that, but there's nowhere to navigate them to.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: And so we need more resources of people to have a crisis bed in that moment, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: Because they're only going to decompensate sitting on that wait list and in that drop-in center.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: And then also being able to move people on to something that's stable and supportive.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Nadia Swanson
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you, folks.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: We'll now move on to the next round.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: We appreciate your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'd like to

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Call up, I have ex-Pamela Farkour, Henry Love, Timothy Pena, David Miranda, and Mateo Guerrero.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Anyone can begin when ready.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Chetanya Gunrathi
utterances.text: You can begin.

chapters.summary: Timothy Pena, a Navy veteran and former Borden Avenue Veterans Residence resident, testifies about 1,365 emergency calls in 16 months (one every nine hours), safety concerns, and witnessing veteran overdoses. He raises these issues at this hearing because other committees have not taken his allegations seriously.
chapters.title: Timothy Pena: Veteran shelter conditions at Borden Avenue

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: My name is Timothy Pena.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: I'm a service-connected

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Navy veteran with PTSD.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: I'm also a former resident of Borden Avenue Veterans Residence, which is a veterans program, transitional program in Long Island City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: I resided at Borden Avenue Veterans Residence in 2022 while participating in New York City's homeless services system and pursuing permanent housing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: During my time at Borden, I observed frequent ambulance and police activity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Residents regularly discussed concerns involving safety, mental health issues, substance abuse, and living conditions within the facility.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: I was concerned.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: So I spent five months at Borden Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: I'm in supportive housing now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: I've been there for about three and a half years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: And my concerns are the conditions in the shelters, especially for veterans in the VA system.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: And I did it in a FOIA that I did with Borden Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: This is the only program that we have available to us for us that are in the VA system.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: In a 16-month period of time,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: 24 to 25, there were 1,365 emergency calls, one every nine hours.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: This is also at considerable cost to the city, somewhere in the neighborhood of $20,000 per person.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Per year.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I appreciate

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Appreciate you coming in to give both of our committees your testimony and the facts here.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I read through and understand that you are raising it here today because you want more folks to be aware and the other committees have not.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: As in your words, taking the allegation seriously, we will make sure to raise it to the commissioner and to raise it to our Veterans Committee Chair as well.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: But I just wanted to state, you know, I understand why you're here today giving us this testimony.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And we'll make sure to raise it to the appropriate folks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: I do want to just thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: It's not easy being HIV positive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: As a veteran in a program, in a shelter like that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: It doesn't take much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: I was pretty terrified for the five months that I was at Borden.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: New York City is an incredible city, but that was my introduction.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: To New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: And it's the introduction that a lot of us are getting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: And it's not fair.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: It's not fair to New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: It's not fair to New Yorkers to have a situation like that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: And I watched lots of, you know, I've watched veterans die of overdose.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: And this is the same as happening in a VA hospital.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: So those...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: These are the same type of applications that are supposed to apply.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: So thank you very much for hearing my testimony.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Dr. Henry Love shares personal stories of losing his best friend Cordell and partner Devon to housing instability-related deaths, both before age 30. He describes how housing instability raises suicide attempts 3.7 times and disrupts HIV treatment for the 20% of homeless youth living with HIV.
chapters.title: Henry Love (Point Source Youth): Personal loss and housing instability

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: I'm a child of Stonewall, a child of Marsha and Sylvia.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: My name is Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: Henry Love, and many of you know me from my time at Wynn, Women in Need, but today I'm here for something a little different.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: I now lead the Targeted Housing Instances Program at Point Source Youth, a national organization focused on ending LGBT youth homelessness.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: As a queer black man and a person living with HIV, an unfathomable trauma.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: I've done the decorum, I have done the degrees, I've done the politeness.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: Today I'm going to be real, I'm going to apologize if it makes you feel a little bit uncomfortable, but that's the point.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: I'm here as living proof of what happens when support arrives on time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: This testimony is for the ones who did not have that opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: Earlier, the young people spoke about the data and the stats, the deaths.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: And in our community, we have a saying, we survived Atlanta.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: It marks who made it and it memorializes those of us who didn't make it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: Cordell Fowler is my best friend.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: In 2016, his funeral was in Detroit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: He was the it girl of Atlanta, magnetic, brilliant.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: And in our last conversation at the Alof Hotel in Harlem, he spoke about how he wanted to move here and be a part of this city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: He was surviving through sex work, begging for help, and was turned away.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: And he ultimately died in 2016 in a drug-related accident.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: But don't call it a drug-related accident.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: It was related to housing instability, and that's ultimately what led to his death.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: In 2017, Devon Way passed away, and his funeral was in Houston.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: Devon was my partner, my soulmate, my safe place in an unsafe world.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: He was from the deep south, and he made it out to Columbia University.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: One month from completing his PhD, he was taken too soon from us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: When I found out about Cordell's death, Devon carried me, and weeks later when I received my HIV diagnosis, Devon carried me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: And then he was gone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: Sorry, I'm almost done.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: He was killed by someone who was housing unstable, untreated,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: And one month from that finish line, Frank in Arkansas, James in Virginia, Jeremy Delano from Detroit, people I saw every Thursday at Trappie Hall in Harlem.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: I made it out, but so many didn't.

chapters.summary: Love describes the TAP program, which provides approximately $3,700 in direct cash to youth within 48 hours, with 98% of recipients having no shelter contact in six months per Johns Hopkins evaluation. He urges the council to fund the $4.7M city council request for TAP.
chapters.title: Henry Love: Targeted Housing Assistance Program (TAP) proposal

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: So why TAP?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: Why the Targeting Housing Instances Program?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: LGBT youth, as we earlier mentioned, are 40% of the homeless youth and only 9% of the homeless youth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: of the population.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: Housing instability raises suicide attempts by 3.7 times, and up to 20% of homeless youth in NYC are living with HIV.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: They can't maintain HIV treatment, they can't stay connected to care, and TAP creates the stability that makes survival possible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: TAP is simple.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: 98% of those who participate in the TAP program, which on average gives about $3,700 in direct cash to young people within 48 hours to stabilize them,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: 98% of them, of recipients at Henry Street and at the door, had no shelter contact in six months, per our evaluation at Johns Hopkins University.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: So, in the time, I asked that Cordell and Devon's obituaries are here on the table as just proof of what happens to young people who don't make it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: Neither of them made it past 30.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: $4.7 million is being asked by the city council to fund TAP, the Targeted Housing Assistance Program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: It's imperative that we do this now and to show that we are fully human.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
