utterances.text: And in the end of that, I want to show you just this tattoo.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: As I was doing this, I thought about it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: Devon had this same tattoo here, and it says empathy, and that's what his dissertation was about.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: And I ask that as you are in these final conversations on the budget, that you think about all the young people, and you think about Devon, you think about Cordell, who didn't make it past 30 because they didn't have someone to intervene.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: They didn't have policy to intervene for them in time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Henry Love
utterances.text: I stopped there.

chapters.summary: David Miranda of Manhattan Legal Services describes a client who waited five years for a HASA voucher because nobody told her the rent was too low, forcing her and her autistic daughter into shelters. He calls for fixing administrative delays and transparency failures within HASA.
chapters.title: David Miranda (Legal Services NYC): HASA barriers and housing failures

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: Good afternoon, everybody.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: My name is David Miranda, and I'm an attorney with the LGBTQ Plus and HIV Advocacy Project at Manhattan Legal Services, which is part of Legal Services NYC.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: We have offices in all five boroughs, a dedicated team of advocates providing services specific to LGBTQIA folks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: Many of whom experience housing instability, poverty, and discrimination.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: New York City's housing affordability crisis affects residents across the city, but LGBTQIA plus New Yorkers often face additional barriers that exacerbate housing insecurity, including pervasive discrimination,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: limited access to affirming housing and shelter environments, and administrative obstacles within public benefits.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: Today, I will discuss the HIV AIDS Services Administration, known as HASA, for case management and rental assistance, the homeless

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: crisis among LGBTQIA plus youth, and the failure of demographic data to capture the severity of this crisis.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: I'm going to start by talking about HASA, and I do think that there are several issues within the HASA program, which is operated by HRA.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: We have seen that housing opportunities are often lost due to administrative delays within the HASA program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: There is also a

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: a severe lack of transparency, which means that clients either lose housing or are unable to get the housing that they deserve.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: And by way of example, I'm going to talk about a client who we shall call Ms.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: W.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: Ms.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: W is from the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: And she had been trying to get a HASA housing voucher for five years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: She identifies as a lesbian, and she reached out to us because she could not handle another denial.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: During those five years from the time of her application to the time when we finally were able to resolve the issue, she and her daughter, who suffers from severe autism, were forced into the shelter system.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: Nobody ever told Ms.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: W at HASA that the issue was that the rent was too low, even though she had found three apartments within New York City NYC Connect.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: And every time she would get rejected because the rent was too low.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: So her and her daughter would go from shelter to shelter and couch to couch.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: And that is a fundamental failure within a system that we can actually fix.

chapters.summary: Miranda highlights the foster care-to-homelessness pipeline for LGBTQ+ youth, with over one-third of foster youth identifying as LGBTQ+. He also argues that NYC's data collection misses hidden homelessness, including couch surfing and informal arrangements, requiring street-level outreach to capture.
chapters.title: David Miranda: Youth homelessness, foster care pipeline, and data gaps

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: Moving on to the youth shelter, I would not, to youth homelessness, I would not, a conversation about housing instability within this community would not be complete if we did not talk about youth homelessness.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: Without familiar support, the city must provide additional support to ensure

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: ensure that youth are valued, supported, and have the opportunity to create a better life for themselves.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: Additionally, LGBTQIA youth are disproportionately represented in the foster care system, with more than one-third of youth identifying as LGBTQIA, and then there aren't systems in place

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: to ensure that they can go into stable housing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: Instead, there's a foster care to homelessness pipeline.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: And onto the final topic, New York City's demographic data collection creates a hidden homelessness crisis.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: There are many more people who are LGBTQIA+,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: who do not have stable housing, who are not in shelter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: And if we're only counting by who's in shelter, then we're missing those folks who are out there couch surfing or sleeping on the train or going from friend to friend or in houses, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: Like in sort of those less traditional,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: where there might be some more stability that could be provided.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: So we do have to figure out ways to measure that data.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: I think it could be done.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: It's going to take a lot of street walking, literally, like walking on the street and hitting that pavement and talking to community members.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: And I think that's important.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: And just to wrap up, I just want to thank the council for your attention to this issue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: I urge the council to ensure that the agencies serving LGBTQIA New Yorkers, including HRA, DHS, and DYCD, fulfill their mission.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: of providing safe, affirming, and equitable services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: Pride must be more than a celebration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: It must be reflected in policies and programs that protect the housing stability, health, and dignity of LGBTQIA plus New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - David Miranda
utterances.text: And again, this hearing speaks volumes to that, that at least we're somewhat

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: the one of the way there.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: So I thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And I thank you for the opportunity to testify today.

chapters.summary: Mateo Guerrero shares three stories: a trans migrant couple assaulted at a shelter where staff called police instead of de-escalating; a trans elder who believed HIV infection was her only housing pathway; and a queer migrant illegally locked out by their landlord with police assistance at the 102nd precinct.
chapters.title: Mateo Guerrero (Make the Road NY): Trans migrant housing stories

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: Hi, good afternoon, everybody.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Hudson and Chair Farias, for the opportunity to testify today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: My name is Mateo Guerrero.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: I work at Make the World New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: I'm the Trans Justice and Leadership Program Manager.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: And I'm also trans myself and a migrant myself, and I get to work with those communities who are at the intersection of police harassment, housing instability, employment discrimination, immigration processes, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: I want to share three stories before I name my demands, my asks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: The first story, this is one that we hear very common.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: One of our members, whom I'm going to refer to as E, she's a trans woman, a migrant trans woman, and her partner, K, a queer migrant man.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: They were assaulted at a shelter by other people, housed at the same facility, based on their gender identity and their sexual orientation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: The staff at the shelter, instead of providing support to intervene or providing support to de-escalate, they called the police and everybody involved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: When the police arrived, they did not provide access to interpretation, and they ultimately arrested Kay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: This case was dismissed in court, but it could have resulted in immigration complications for Kay as he was adjusting his immigration status.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: In this case, we see multiple systemic failures.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: occurring at once.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: One, the discrimination based on gender identity and sexual orientation, the denial of language access by police, as well as police interaction that can exacerbate complications for their immigration status.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: The second story is M, someone who we love very deeply in our community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: She is one of our trans migrant elders.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: M, she was very tired of waiting for Section 8 voucher or any other housing subsidy from the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: And she came to believe that the only way to accessing stable housing was becoming HIV positive.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: M has been doing sex work for more than four decades.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: And as someone who is aging in the industry, she found herself without a reliable source of income.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: And so she felt that this was the only pathway to prevent herself from being unhoused again.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: And I'm also going to share the story of F.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: He was illegally locked out of their basement apartment just two weeks ago.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: This was on June 11th.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: F landlord has been denying them the access, the opportunity to bring friends over, even though this was a separate unit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: They were denying them of a lease, denying them of proof of payment of rent, denying them of receiving letters or any proof of residence in their home.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: And they have been harassed because of their sexual orientation and gender identity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: On June 11th is when the landlord decided to lock F.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: out of the basement apartment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: And the police helped the landlord without giving an opportunity to F to be able to share what had happened and that they actually live in that apartment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: They deny access to interpretation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: And by the way, this was the 102nd precinct.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: And they changed the locks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: Since then, F has been couch surfing until this resolves in court.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: But they are terrified of going into a city shelter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: They were housed at a male shelter in the past and was very harassed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: And so he does not want they don't want to return there.

chapters.summary: Guerrero urges the council to pressure the Mamdani administration to end the lawsuit against CityFHEPS expansion, expand the Housing Access Voucher Program beyond 900 vouchers, and support the NYC Trans and Queer Coalition's budget demands including $15M for gender-affirming care and $10M for trans equity.
chapters.title: Mateo Guerrero: Policy demands for CityFHEPS and housing vouchers

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: And these are stories that are not unique for our communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: We hear this very often in our Trans-Migrant Project.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: We know that there is a housing crisis in New York City, but for trans and queer migrant New Yorkers, this is a crisis that is compounded by multiple systems of discrimination that include exposure to violence, instability, criminalization, and displacement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: And so the two asks that I want to make is, one, I want to ask the council to continue to put pressure on the Mamdani administration to end the lawsuit against the 2023-2020.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: CityFAP's expansion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: That expansion is so important for our communities to be able to have access to these vouchers, to have access to this program, and also that is regardless of immigration status, which is so important for our trans and queer migrants.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: And the second one is to increase funding through the budget process.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: So at the state level, we passed the housing

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: access voucher program, but I also believe that it's the city duty to expand that program and to allocate budget for that program as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: The 900 vouchers that were allocated in New York City are simply not enough when there are around 100,000 people who are facing homelessness and 3 million people who are rent burdened and are in danger of losing their home.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: And this program is intended to support people who are on house as well as people who are at risk of losing their housing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: And lastly, I want to name that as Make the Road, we also deeply support all the budget demands of the New York City Trans and Queer Coalition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: $15 million for gender affirming care for youth, $10 million for trans equity initiative fund, $10 million for support persons involved in the sex trades, and $15 million for LGBTQ immigrant fund, because when we think about housing instability, it's also all these

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: other areas that provide support for our folks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mateo Guerrero
utterances.text: Thank you so much for your time.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you folks for testifying.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: We don't have any questions.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: So this panel is now excused.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I will move to virtual testimony.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: We have three, four.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: We have four folks for virtual testimony.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I'm going to begin with Ateem Aton and Erica Lashbo.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Ateem, if you are available to unmute yourself, you may begin when ready.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Atzamal Tone describes Project Renewal's Marsha's House, NYC's first LGBTQ+ shelter for adults 18-35, offering 81 beds with primary care, mental health, and job readiness programs. In FY26, 28 residents were placed into permanent housing. Tone urges continued investment in LGBTQ+-designed shelter models.
chapters.title: Atzamal Tone (Project Renewal): Marsha's House services and outcomes

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Atzamal Tone
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Atzamal Tone
utterances.text: Good morning, Chairs Hudson and Farias.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Atzamal Tone
utterances.text: Thank you for chairing this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Atzamal Tone
utterances.text: My name is Atzamal Tone.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Atzamal Tone
utterances.text: I am the Nurse Care Manager for HIV and Gender Affirming Care at Project Renewal.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Atzamal Tone
utterances.text: For over 55 years, we have provided comprehensive shelter, health care, and employment services to New Yorkers experiencing homelessness.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Atzamal Tone
utterances.text: We are deeply grateful to the City Council for its continued commitment to addressing housing instability, particularly among LGBTQIA plus individuals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Atzamal Tone
utterances.text: Project Renewal operates Marsha's House in the Bronx, New York City's first shelter tailored specifically for LGBTQIA plus young adults aged 18 to 35.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Atzamal Tone
utterances.text: These young people need far more than just a bed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Atzamal Tone
utterances.text: Many have faced severe family rejection, trauma, and systemic discrimination.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Atzamal Tone
utterances.text: Traditional shelter environments can often create additional fear and vulnerability if the care provided is not explicitly affirming and responsive to their needs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Atzamal Tone
utterances.text: Marsha's House offers 81 beds and provides crucial coordinated support, including primary health care, mental health services, occupational therapy, and job readiness programs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Atzamal Tone
utterances.text: Our holistic approach delivers tangible results.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Atzamal Tone
utterances.text: In fiscal year 26, we successfully placed 28 residents into permanent long-term housing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Atzamal Tone
utterances.text: We also know that true housing stability requires addressing trauma and access to health simultaneously.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Atzamal Tone
utterances.text: Thanks to the New York City Council, the LGBTQIA Plus Community Services Initiative provides critical support for our gender-affirming primary and behavioral health care to homeless trans and GNC New Yorkers in NYC.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Atzamal Tone
utterances.text: As the council considers housing instability among LGBTQIA plus New Yorkers, we urge continued investments in shelter and housing models that are designed with LGBTQIA plus communities in mind.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Your time expired.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Atzamal Tone
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Atzamal Tone
utterances.text: Thank you for your partnership.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

chapters.summary: Erica Lorish-Baugh of Interact Advocates for Intersex Youth testifies that intersex people report housing discrimination at more than triple the rate of non-intersex people, are four times more likely to need housing assistance, and urges passage of Intro 131 to make anti-discrimination protections for intersex people explicit in city human rights law.
chapters.title: Erica Lorish-Baugh (Interact): Intersex housing discrimination

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I'll now call on Erica Larshbow.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Lorish-Baugh
utterances.text: Good afternoon, my name is Erica Lorish-Baugh, any pronouns are fine, and thanks for the opportunity to speak about drivers of housing instability in the intersex community in New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Lorish-Baugh
utterances.text: I'm a queer, genderqueer, and intersex New Yorker, and the executive director of Interact Advocates for Intersex Youth, the nation's largest intersex advocacy organization.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Lorish-Baugh
utterances.text: Intersex is an umbrella term for people with innate variations in our sex characteristics, such as chromosomes, hormones, and internal or external anatomy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Lorish-Baugh
utterances.text: We don't have city-specific data on housing insecurity to share as a gravely understudied population, but intersex people face the same socioeconomic challenges as other in the community due to inequitable treatment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Lorish-Baugh
utterances.text: Over six years of nationwide surveys, intersex people tend to report higher rates of discrimination than other LGBTQ plus people, and more than triple the rate of people without intersex traits.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Lorish-Baugh
utterances.text: One study found intersex people more than four times as likely to require housing assistance as other LGBTQ plus community members.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Lorish-Baugh
utterances.text: In a recent study, a quarter of intersex people reported housing discrimination in the prior year, compared with 10% of LGBTQ plus people and just 4% of all non-intersex respondents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Lorish-Baugh
utterances.text: Intersex people also report high levels of employment discrimination and are more likely than other community members to have children and to be physically disabled.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Lorish-Baugh
utterances.text: Limited access to larger apartments and ADA-compliant housing are more likely to be compounding factors in housing instability for our community as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Lorish-Baugh
utterances.text: To the extent that in that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Lorish-Baugh
utterances.text: equitable treatment of intersex people drives housing instability, the council does have an opportunity to take action.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Lorish-Baugh
utterances.text: Councilmember Cabam's bill, Intro 131, would make anti-discrimination protections for intersex people explicit on the face of the city human rights law.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Lorish-Baugh
utterances.text: Justice Director Taylor Brown stated earlier today, making these protections plain will help intersex New Yorkers understand their rights and enforce them and underscore the importance for employers and housing providers as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Lorish-Baugh
utterances.text: For a community that disproportionately experiences discrimination, clarity in the law can make a real difference, especially given that the intersex community is often overlooked when considering the broader LGBTQ plus community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Lorish-Baugh
utterances.text: We urge the council to hear and pass Intro 131.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Lorish-Baugh
utterances.text: Thanks for your time and consideration, and I'll do my best to answer any questions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Erica Lorish-Baugh
utterances.text: Always feel free to follow up with us at Interact.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I'll now call on Peyton Moody.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I will now call on Ramona Ferreira.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Chair Hudson calls on two additional virtual witnesses who do not respond, then closes the public testimony portion and adjourns the hearing.
chapters.title: Hearing adjourned after no further testimony

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Seeing no others both here in person or virtual looking to testify,

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: we will now close the public portion for testimony, and this hearing is now adjourned.

summary: Joint oversight hearing of the Committees on Women and Gender Equity and General Welfare examining housing instability among LGBTQ+ individuals in NYC, featuring testimony from the Mayor's Office of LGBTQIA Affairs, DSS, and DYCD.

Council members questioned agencies on shelter safety, TGNC-specific beds, staff training, data collection gaps, HASA services, and the Lopez settlement, while advocates urged funding for housing vouchers, LGBTQ+ youth beds, and immigrant legal services.

Public testimony included firsthand accounts from formerly homeless LGBTQ+ youth, advocates from Ali Forney Center and SAGE, and calls for a $15M LGBTQ+ immigration fund and $4.7M targeted housing assistance program.
title: Committee on General Welfare
